elements.sent-2411-001: The agent explores its universe and learns concepts of objects/situations and of its own actions in a one-shot manner.
elements.sent-2411-002: While object/situation concepts are unary, action concepts are triples made up of an initial situation, a motor activity, and an outcome.
elements.sent-2411-003: They embody the agent's knowledge of its universe's actions laws.
elements.sent-2411-004: Both kinds of concepts have different degrees of generality.
elements.sent-2411-005: To make decisions the agent queries its semantic memory for the expected outcomes of envisaged actions and chooses the action to take on the basis of these predictions.
elements.sent-2411-006: Our experiments show that the agent handles new situations by appealing to previously learned general concepts and rapidly modifies its concepts to adapt to environment changes.. \end{onecolabstract ].}
elements.sent-2411-007: The ability of a cognitive agent to act adequately in a given environment depends on its ability to predict how performing a given activity might affect its current situation, that is, it depends on its knowledge of its environment's action laws.
elements.sent-2411-008: How artificial agents can acquire these laws and how these should be updated if their environment changes has proven a difficult question.
elements.sent-2411-009: In the case where the intended environment is open---that is, where the agent's designer cannot foresee all the situations the agent might encounter in the future---, providing a suitable set of action laws to the agent ``by hand'' is unfeasible.
elements.sent-2411-010: The only viable solution is that the agent continuously learns the relevant laws from experience, just as natural agents (humans and animals) do.
elements.sent-2411-011: Crucially, this learning process should allow for generalization over disparate experiences, so that the agent is able to behave appropriately in new situations.
elements.sent-2411-012: It should also accommodate environment changes.
elements.sent-2411-013: The present paper intends to show how this could be done.
elements.sent-2411-014: Its main thrust is that natural agents' ability to perform well in our open and changing world relies on the fact that they store their knowledge in the form of concepts, which can have various degrees of generality.
elements.sent-2411-015: Presumably, they first form concepts about the encountered objects and situations, and then use these as building blocks for relational concepts, among which are concepts of actions supporting their knowledge of their environment's action laws.
elements.sent-2411-016: We suggest that artificial agents could do just the same, relying on some artificial neural network to learn and store concepts, and then querying it to make predictions about the outcome of envisaged actions.
elements.sent-2411-017: To test this idea, we here build an artificial hybrid agent with a SNN at its core.
elements.sent-2411-018: We make this agent live in a very simple virtual world, composed of rooms which may be, or not, accessible (hence, knowable) to it.
elements.sent-2411-019: At first, the agent is confined to one single room and learns by itself how to act in it according to its own interests.
elements.sent-2411-020: Then at some point a door opens to a new room, containing some never encountered before objects and situations.
elements.sent-2411-021: Yet, although these are new to the agent, some general laws are preserved from one room to the other.
elements.sent-2411-022: We show that having learned these laws in the first room allows the agent to act by and large properly in the second one, as soon as it enters it.
elements.sent-2411-023: We also show that relying on neurally implemented concepts allows the agent to rapidly update its knowledge and adapt to environment changes.
elements.sent-2411-024: The paper is organized as follows.
elements.sent-2411-025: In Section 2 we discuss related work, and in Section 3 we present the agent and its universe.
elements.sent-2411-026: Section 4 clarifies the notions of concepts and actions laws we use, while Section 5 describes the neural network and its functioning.
elements.sent-2411-027: Section 6 describes the agent's general functioning and Section 7 presents the results.
elements.sent-2411-028: Finally, Section 8 concludes and discusses future possible developments of the framework.
elements.sent-2411-029: Our research problem is autonomous online learning, generalization and updating of concepts and actions laws in an open universe.
elements.sent-2411-030: The intended application is reasoning and planning for autonomous robots.
elements.sent-2411-031: To our knowledge, no existing approach addresses this problem in all its dimensions, even though these are investigated in separate research fields.
elements.sent-2411-032: Continual Learning tackles the problem of lifelong knowledge acquisition \cite{WanEtAl24, Les20 .}
elements.sent-2411-033: Its main challenge is to avoid catastrophic loss of previous knowledge when acquiring new knowledge; a secondary research axis is one-shot/few-shots learning, i.e., the ability to learn online from one or few examples \cite{WanEtAl20 .}
elements.sent-2411-034: However, current approaches mostly consider the learning of tasks (mostly image classification/recognition tasks, but also some more complex tasks such as playing games \cite{KirEtAl17 ), not of concepts nor action laws.}
elements.sent-2411-035: Furthermore, most of them rely on supervised learning and/or labelled training data, which is unsuitable for open world autonomous agents.
elements.sent-2411-036: Concept Learning has mainly been studied in view of explainability \cite{Gup2024 , mostly of classification models (e.g., \cite{Koh2020 ) but also of decision making in the context of reinforcement learning \cite{DasEtAl2023, ZabEtAl2023 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-037: For this reason, many proposals are dedicated to learning a human-predefined set of concepts using some annotated data.
elements.sent-2411-038: In the field of Image Classification some approaches deal with the extraction of concepts from data \cite{WanEtAl2022, Gho19, Has2019 , but in these approaches concepts are extracted from labelled classes of images, which is, again, unsuitable for open world autonomous agents.}
elements.sent-2411-039: Furthermore, the vast majority of proposed methods disregard the hierarchical organization of concepts from particular to more general, and they generally do not address one-shot learning, online revision or updating of concepts.
elements.sent-2411-040: Action Learning has been studied from various perspectives.
elements.sent-2411-041: In Dynamic Epistemic Logic, \cite{Bol18 proposed a method to learn an action model through successive observations of transitions between states.}
elements.sent-2411-042: However this method does not achieve generalization nor accommodate environment changes and only considers the universally applicable actions (i.e., actions that can be executed in every logically possible state), a condition real-world actions rarely satisfy.
elements.sent-2411-043: In the field of Planning, \cite{BonEtAl2019 showed how to learn abstract actions from a few carefully chosen instances of some general planning problem.}
elements.sent-2411-044: However said instances come with their own set of ground actions which must be known beforehand, hence this approach cannot be used in open worlds, where an agent needs to incrementally learn from sequential observations.
elements.sent-2411-045: Reinforcement Learning (RL): Our work could be related to model-based approaches of RL \cite{Moe2023 , but differs from them in two important aspects.}
elements.sent-2411-046: First, we are only interested in learning a model of the environment, not in learning policies.
elements.sent-2411-047: Despite their undeniable successes, RL approaches struggle to adapt to environment changes and to revise learned policies \cite{Kir2023, Far2018 .}
elements.sent-2411-048: We believe that an agent that would be able to learn online a model of the environment and to dynamically use it to make decisions would be able to quickly adapt its behavior.
elements.sent-2411-049: Second, the learning process we propose does not depend on the existence of rewards, which makes it suitable for contexts where rewards are scarce.
elements.sent-2411-050: The agent's universe is built over a grid of boxes, which we (not the agent) identify using an orthonormal coordinate system (see Figure~\ref{fig:Universe ).}
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=1\columnwidth]{Universe.jpg} \caption{The agent's accessible world. A: in the first phase, Room 1 only; B: after opening the door, Rooms 1 and 2.} \label{fig:Universe}
elements.sent-2411-051: Each box represents a particular location in the agent's universe and possesses a particular set of features the agent is able to perceive, drawn from the set \locFeat\ = $\{$\OKFname, \KOFname, \NWallFname, \EWallFname, \SWallFname, \WWallFname, \ColdFname, \SoundFname, \CaseNbZeroFname, \CaseNbOneFname, ..., \CaseNbTwentyFourFname $ \}$.
elements.sent-2411-052: Although the agent's universe is not finite, at any time point we only consider the boxes to which it has access, the set of which is always finite.
elements.sent-2411-053: For example, the box with coordinates $(-2, -2)$ has the feature set \locFeat$_{(-2, -2) = \{$\OKFname, \SWallFname, \WWallFname, \ColdFname, \CaseNbZeroFname $ \}$, while the box with coordinates $(5, 0)$ has the feature set \locFeat$_{(5, 0) = \{$\OKFname$\}$ (``\#n'' is to be taken as a particular name for a box hence a feature, not all boxes need to have one).}}
elements.sent-2411-054: Two boxes with the same feature set are indistinguishable for the agent.
elements.sent-2411-055: Boxes' feature sets may change over time, reflecting environment changes.
elements.sent-2411-056: The rooms are made out of boxes, and delimited with impassable walls.
elements.sent-2411-057: Opening a door amounts to removing the wall features from the concerned boxes' feature sets (as in Figure~\ref{fig:Universe .B).}
elements.sent-2411-058: The agent is composed of a set of sensors, a perceptual system, a semantic memory, a decision system, a motor system and a set of actuators (see Figure~\ref{fig:Schema Agent ).}
elements.sent-2411-059: Sensors collect data from the external world and feed it to the perceptual system, which performs feature/object recognition.
elements.sent-2411-060: Since we want the agent to learn by itself, we need this process to rely on unsupervised learning with unlabelled data.
elements.sent-2411-061: Neural networks doing this already exist \cite{ThiEtAl18, Khe17 , so we simply suppose that the agent's perceptual system operates as intended and provides the semantic memory with the appropriate inputs, namely, the features of the agent's current location.}
elements.sent-2411-062: Furthermore, we suppose that the agent's observations are always correct and complete.
elements.sent-2411-063: Semantic memory forms concepts by binding together the perceived features, and stores them for further retrieval.
elements.sent-2411-064: Its modeling is the main focus of this paper.
elements.sent-2411-065: The decision system is the other important part: it queries the semantic memory to predict the outcome of possible actions, and decides which one to take on the basis of these predictions.
elements.sent-2411-066: This decision is then sent to the motor system, which activates the actuators to perform the corresponding motor activity.
elements.sent-2411-067: Information from the actuators is sent back to semantic memory through proprioception, allowing the agent to memorize the motor-related features of the realized actions.
elements.sent-2411-068: The agent's possible actions consist in steps from one box to another adjacent box, in any of the eight directions.
elements.sent-2411-069: Formally, an action is a triple composed of a depart location, a motor activity, and an outcome.
elements.sent-2411-070: By ``motor activity'' we mean the fact that the agent's actuators are activated so as to make it move to the immediate next box in the selected direction.
elements.sent-2411-071: The set of motor activities' features the agent is able to perceive by proprioception is the set \motFeat\ = $\{$\NDeplFname, \NEDeplFname, \EDeplFname, \SEDeplFname, \SDeplFname, \SWDeplFname, \WDeplFname, \NWDeplFname, \DiagFname, \OrthFname$\}$, where the first eight are specific to each particular direction, while \DiagFname\ and \OrthFname\ are common features shared by all motor activities yielding diagonal/orthogonal moves.
elements.sent-2411-072: In cases where there is a wall at the edge of the depart box in the selected direction, the agent bumps into it and remains at the same place.
elements.sent-2411-073: We then say that the action's outcome is a failure.
elements.sent-2411-074: Otherwise, the action's outcome is the agent's new location.
elements.sent-2411-075: As for the locations' features, we suppose that \KOFname\ corresponds to some unpleasant stimulus the agent spontaneously wants to avoid, and \OKFname\ to the absence of such a stimulus, while the others convey some indifferent information.
elements.sent-2411-076: The agent is able to form two kinds of concepts.
elements.sent-2411-077: First, concepts of ``things'', in the broad sense.
elements.sent-2411-078: These bind together co-occurrent features, and can be seen as some sort of conjunction in which conjuncts have different ``weights'', reflecting the fact that some features are more important than others in a concept's definition \cite{Fre08 .}
elements.sent-2411-079: They are used to store knowledge about locations and more generally any object, so we call them object concepts.
elements.sent-2411-080: The second kind is relational concepts.
elements.sent-2411-081: These take other concepts as elements, and bind them together into tuples.
elements.sent-2411-082: Concepts of actions are of this kind: they bind together the agent's concepts of a depart location, a performed motor activity, and a subsequent outcome, in the order in which they were experienced.
elements.sent-2411-083: We say that an object concept X is general, as opposed to particular, if there is another concept Y such that the set of features composing X is a strict subset of the set of features composing Y.
elements.sent-2411-084: Y is then said to be more particular than X.
elements.sent-2411-085: We say that an action concept is general if the object concept of its initial situation is general or its motor activity component only contains \DiagFname\ or \OrthFname.
elements.sent-2411-086: We understand the generality of concepts relative to the set of concepts the agent possesses at some point, so no concept is general or particular in itself.
elements.sent-2411-087: For example, when visiting the box $(0, 0)$ the agent may form the particular object concept \cpt{OK,\#12 , which is a memory of an OK place with name \#12, and only applies to this particular box in its accessible universe.}
elements.sent-2411-088: If it then moves North-East and arrives at box $(1, 1)$, it can form the particular object concept \cpt{OK,\#18 , and also the particular action concept \cpt{\cpt{OK,\#12 ,\cpt{NE,Diag ,\cpt{OK,\#18 which corresponds to the memory of being in an OK place with name \#12 and then moving North-East to arrive at another OK place with name \#18.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-089: Yet, after visiting a number of locations having the feature \feat{OK in common, the agent may also form the general object concept \cpt{OK .}}
elements.sent-2411-090: Furthermore, it is a general rule in its accessible universe that moving North-East from an OK location always leads to another OK location, except for when there is a wall at the North or East edge of the depart box.
elements.sent-2411-091: Therefore, after having experienced a number of North-East moves from various OK locations, the agent may form general action concepts such as \cpt{\cpt{OK ,\cpt{NE,Diag ,\cpt{OK and \cpt{\cpt{OK,NorthWall}\cpt{NE,Diag ,\cpt{Failure .}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-092: Such general concepts capture the general (non-monotonic) action laws of the agent's universe, and are the ones it shall rely on to behave in never encountered situations.
elements.section-2411-005: Implementing the Agent's Semantic Memory in the Neural Network
elements.sent-2411-093: Spiking Neural Networks (SNNs) are well suited for autonomous learning in open universes, as they allow for Spike Time Dependent Plasticity (STDP), a family of biologically plausible learning rules which can achieve unsupervised online learning from unlabelled data \cite{ThiEtAl18 .}
elements.sent-2411-094: They are also known for being energy-efficient, which is interesting for autonomous robots.
elements.sent-2411-095: We take inspiration in the JAST learning rule \cite{ThoEtAl19, Tor23 , which is a simplified version of STDP where the sum of the afferent connections weights on any given neuron remains constant through learning.}
elements.sent-2411-096: However, contrary to JAST we do not use binary weights but natural numbers.
elements.sent-2411-097: Moreover, we do not freeze neurons after learning, so as to allow updating.
elements.sent-2411-098: The network is composed of an interface, which communicates with the agent's other components, and a body of hidden neurons which is itself divided into two layers (see Figure~\ref{fig:SNN ).}
elements.figure-2411-003: \includegraphics[width=1\columnwidth]{SNN.jpg} \caption{Schema of the SNN. O-neuron \#1 supports the concept \cpt{\#0,\,Cold,\,SouthWall,\,WestWall,\,OK}, and O-neuron \#2 the concept \cpt{\#5,\,SouthWall,\,KO}. A-neuron \#1 supports the concept \cpt{\cpt{\#0,\,Cold, SouthWall,\,WestWall,\,OK},\,\cpt{E,\,Orth},\,\cpt{\#5,\,South\-Wall,\,KO}}, and A-neuron \#3 the concept \cpt{\cpt{OK}, \cpt{Diag},\,\cpt{OK}}.} \label{fig:SNN}
elements.sent-2411-099: The first layer learns object concepts and the second learns action concepts.
elements.sent-2411-100: For this reason we call their neurons, respectively, object concept neurons (O-neurons for short) and action concept neurons (A-neurons).
elements.sent-2411-101: This architecture draws on neuroanatomical studies according to which concepts are represented in the brain by hierarchically organized concept neurons, each receiving information from some lower neurons and sending reciprocal connections to these same neurons so that it can reactivate them for information retrieval \cite{Qui12, BauEtAl21, Shi10 .}
elements.sent-2411-102: For simplicity we do not model these reciprocal connections as such, but instead we allow for information to flow in both directions along the same connections: from interface neurons to O-neurons and then to A-neurons for learning and querying, and the other way round for retrieving information.
elements.sent-2411-103: A key point is that interface neurons are both input and output neurons, depending on the computational phase.
elements.sent-2411-104: Interface neurons (I-neurons for short) mainly support the representation of features, be it of the visited locations or of the agent's own motor activities.
elements.sent-2411-105: An additional neuron acts as a failure detector, specifically firing when the agent bumps into a wall and remains at the same place.
elements.sent-2411-106: All of them have their labels fixed from the start.
elements.sent-2411-107: The first layer of hidden neurons is composed of 100 integrate and fire neurons, with a differentiated dynamics depending on whether their input source is I-neurons or A-neurons.
elements.sent-2411-108: O-neurons learn co-occurrences of perceived features.
elements.sent-2411-109: The second layer is composed of 400 compartment neurons with three separate input compartments.
elements.sent-2411-110: The first compartment receives connections from O-neurons, the second one from motor activities I-neurons, and the third one from O-neurons and Failure neuron.
elements.sent-2411-111: Inputs received at each compartment are unable to trigger a spike by themselves, but the first and second compartments make their next compartment ready to receive and transmit inputs for a certain amount of time.
elements.sent-2411-112: In this manner, inputs can only be efficient if they occur in the correct order, so that A-neurons encode sequences of inputs.
elements.sent-2411-113: The use of compartment neurons to learn sequences was suggested in \cite{Cui16,HawEtAl16 .}
elements.sent-2411-114: We briefly describe the network's functioning.
elements.sent-2411-115: A more detailed account is provided in Supplementary Material.
elements.sent-2411-116: Each time the agent observes its current location, information from the perceptual system is sent to the network's interface, inducing the firing of the I-neurons that encode the location's features.
elements.sent-2411-117: This in turn triggers the firing of a number of O-neurons.
elements.sent-2411-118: If this number reaches some fixed target number, the network directly proceeds to make them learn.
elements.sent-2411-119: Otherwise it looks for additional O-neurons by ``boosting'' their input.
elements.sent-2411-120: In practice, boosting consists in multiplying the input received by each non-firing O-neuron $o$ by a factor $b_o$, which is an increasing function of the number of steps performed since $o$'s last spike.
elements.sent-2411-121: This procedure favors the firing of O-neurons that have been inactive for a long time, which are then re-used for learning.
elements.sent-2411-122: The learning process depends on the accuracy of the agent's knowledge about its current location.
elements.sent-2411-123: To assess it, the (pre-boosting) firing O-neurons send a backward input to I-neurons, and the resulting set of firing I-neurons (the retrieved information) is compared with the initial input (the current observation).
elements.sent-2411-124: If all the observed features can be retrieved from active O-neurons, then all inactive I-synapses (if any) on the learning O-neurons are deleted and replaced with synapses from input neurons.
elements.sent-2411-125: This procedure tends to reinforce O-neurons' connections with I-neurons encoding well shared features at the expense of connections with I-neurons encoding more specific features, fostering the learning of general concepts.
elements.sent-2411-126: If, on the contrary, not all the observed features can be retrieved from the firing O-neurons, then we pick one learning O-neuron with maximal number of steps since its last learning and replace all its synapses, active or inactive, with synapses from input neurons.
elements.sent-2411-127: This neuron thus learns the particular situation with all its features.
elements.sent-2411-128: The learning process is similar to the first case for the other learning neurons.
elements.sent-2411-129: To query the neural network, the decision system first sends an input to the I-neurons that encode the features of the initial situation.
elements.sent-2411-130: Their firing brings a number of O-neurons to fire, sending an input to A-neurons' first compartment.
elements.sent-2411-131: Then, given an envisaged motor activity $m$, the decision system sends an input to the I-neurons that encode $m$'s features.
elements.sent-2411-132: These fire, and send an input to A-neurons' second compartment.
elements.sent-2411-133: A-neurons' spiking threshold is then gradually lowered until a target number of them fire, sending a backward input to O-neurons through their third compartment's connections.
elements.sent-2411-134: The O-neurons that fire in response to that input in turn send a backward input to I-neurons, the firing of which is the network's response to the query.
elements.sent-2411-135: The value of A-neurons' spiking threshold at the moment a given I-neuron spikes determines the agent's confidence in the feature's prediction: the higher its value, the higher the confidence.
elements.sent-2411-136: So, formally, the querying process returns a set $P_m = \{(f_1, c_1), ...}
elements.sent-2411-137: At each step, O-neurons responding to the depart location and I-neurons responding to the performed motor activity send inputs to, respectively, A-neurons' first and second compartments.
elements.sent-2411-138: A-neurons reaching a certain threshold are selected for learning.
elements.sent-2411-139: If their number reaches some fixed target number, the network directly proceeds to make them learn, otherwise it looks for additional A-neurons by boosting their input in a way similar to the one used for O-neurons.
elements.sent-2411-140: For each learning A-neuron $a$ we compute a learning rate \LR$_a$, which is an increasing function of the number of steps performed since the neuron's last learning.
elements.sent-2411-141: The idea is that seldom used neurons tend to encode more particular concepts than often used neurons, and should thus be able to learn more rapidly to retain more features from a given situation.
elements.sent-2411-142: The learning process depends on the accuracy of the agent's predictions relative to the action's outcome (see Section \ref{sec: agent's functioning ).}
elements.sent-2411-143: If these are correct (i.e., all the expected features are actually present) and complete (i.e., all the actually present features were expected), then the learning process replaces all inactive synapses in $a$'s first two compartments with synapses from their respective input neurons, but only $min($\LR$_a, i)$ in the third one, where $i$ is the number of inactive synapses.
elements.sent-2411-144: If they are not correct, then all inactive synapses plus some active ones are replaced in $a$'s first two compartments, with $max($\LR$_a, i)$ the number of synapses replaced in each one.
elements.sent-2411-145: The learning process for the third compartment is the same as in the first case.
elements.sent-2411-146: If the agent's predictions are not complete, then the number of replaced synapses is $max($\LR$_a, i)$ in all three compartments.
elements.sent-2411-147: This differentiated learning process aims at promoting generalization when predictions are correct and complete while allowing a few neurons to specialize when they are not.
elements.sent-2411-148: Suppose the agent is at some depart location and observes it: information from its perceptual system triggers the firing of the I-neurons encoding the location's features and is transmitted from there to the decision system, which decides to make a step.
elements.sent-2411-149: The agent's choice of a motor activity depends on whether it wants to exploit its current knowledge about the environment, or to explore it to improve its knowledge.
elements.sent-2411-150: The exploration/exploitation dilemma is a well-known problem in online learning \cite{Wat89, SutBar18 , and changing environments make it even more difficult.}
elements.sent-2411-151: We therefore do not try to reach an optimal solution here, but instead we simply make the agent's decision system choose at random, with equal probability, between an Exploration and an Exploitation mode.
elements.sent-2411-152: This choice being made, for each motor activity $m$ out of the eight possible the decision system queries the semantic memory for the outcome the action having the current location for initial situation and $m$ for motor activity.
elements.sent-2411-153: It then rates each of them for its suitability, by building the sets $\mathit{S}$ (for ``Suitable''), $\mathit{US}$ (``Unsuitable'') and $\mathit{UD}$ (``Undecided''): \begin{compactitem \item $\mathit{S = \{(m, c) \mid (\OKFname, c) \in P_m \}$ \item $\mathit{US = \{(m, c) \mid (\KOFname, c) \in P_m$ or $(\FailFname, c) \in P_m\}$ \item $\mathit{UD = \{m\mid \nexists\ c$ s.t.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-154: In Exploration mode, the agent is willing to take risks and chooses an action with the most uncertain outcome possible: if $\mathit{UD}\neq \emptyset$, it picks one from $\mathit{UD}$, otherwise it goes for one with the least $c$ in $\mathit{S}\cup \mathit{US}$.
elements.sent-2411-155: In Exploitation mode, by contrast, the agent just wants to land on an OK box and to avoid KO boxes and failure as much as possible.
elements.sent-2411-156: So, if $\mathit{S}\neq\emptyset$, it chooses one with the greatest $c$.
elements.sent-2411-157: Otherwise, if $\mathit{UD}\neq\emptyset$, it picks one from $\mathit{UD}$.
elements.sent-2411-158: If both $\mathit{S}$ and $\mathit{UD $ are empty, it chooses one with the least $c$ in $\mathit{US}$.}
elements.sent-2411-159: The decision system then transmits its decision to the motor system to perform the selected motor activity.
elements.sent-2411-160: The I-neurons that encode its features are activated by proprioception and send an input to A-neurons' second compartment.
elements.sent-2411-161: We simulate the agent's move by computing its arrival location and its features.
elements.sent-2411-162: If the agent bumps into a wall, the \FailFname\ neuron fires and sends an input to A-neurons' third compartment, and the agent directly learns the action (see A-neurons' learning above).
elements.sent-2411-163: Otherwise, it first learns relevant object concepts about its new location (see O-neurons' learning above), and then the action.
elements.sent-2411-164: After that, the agent is ready for the next step.
elements.sent-2411-165: To test the agent's learning abilities, we placed it at location $(0,0)$ and prompted it to perform a succession of series of steps, each complete sequence of series of steps being called a trial and consisting in 65536 steps in total.
elements.sent-2411-166: The results we present here are averaged over 50 trials.
elements.sent-2411-167: A first group of tests was carried out with the door kept closed all along, so the agent had no access to the second room.
elements.sent-2411-168: First, we tested its ability to learn an action over one single experience (one-shot learning).
elements.sent-2411-169: To do so, after each step we asked it to redo the prediction that led to the just realized action, and compared this new prediction with the action's actual outcome (seeTable~\ref{tab: Post-Learning percentages ).}
elements.sent-2411-170: We call a prediction Correct and Complete (CC) if the predicted features are exactly those of the arrival location.
elements.sent-2411-171: The table's first line shows the mean percentage of steps leading to a CC post-learning prediction, for each series of steps.
elements.sent-2411-172: The second line (MF for ``Missed Features'') shows the average percentage of features occurrences that the agent failed to predict after learning.
elements.sent-2411-173: The third line (PE for ``Predictions Errors'') shows the average percentage of wrongly predicted features occurrences.

elements.sent-2411-206: In this paper we have designed and implemented a fully autonomous agent that learns action laws online and accommodates environment changes.
elements.sent-2411-207: This agent relies on general concepts to handle new situations and dynamically adjusts its concepts to its current environment.
elements.sent-2411-208: This makes it well suited for open worlds: if a new door were to open to a third room with new objects and laws, it would learn them just as it did for the second room.
elements.sent-2411-209: Of course, this would come at the cost of the forgetting of its least used concepts, but these are precisely the ones it needs the less.
elements.sent-2411-210: In fact, the agent's ability to selectively forget ensures that it will always be able to learn about new environments, by replacing old unused concepts by new useful ones.
elements.sent-2411-211: Further work remains to be done to endow the agent with planning abilities.
elements.sent-2411-212: Notably, a notion of applicable action law would be needed.
elements.sent-2411-213: Intuitively, it seems that an action law represented by an action concept \cpt{x,\,y,\,z should be deemed applicable in a situation $s$ if $s$ satisfies all the features in \cptFeat{x and \cptFeat{z $\neq$ \FailFname.}}}
elements.sent-2411-214: Furthermore, to comply with open world requirements the agent would need to be able to build its own set of possible situations (states) online.
elements.sent-2411-215: The set of its object concepts could probably be used to this end.
elements.sent-2411-216: A cost function should also be added, and the decision system should be augmented so as to handle goals.
elements.sent-2411-217: Additionally, a number of other improvements would be desirable in order to allow the agent to live in more realistic environments.
elements.sent-2411-218: A first one would be to implement negation in the network, so that the agent would be able to represent the fact that a given object does not have a given feature.
elements.sent-2411-219: We believe that this could be done by the means of neural inhibition, but the appropriate learning rules remain to be found.
elements.sent-2411-220: Another essential improvement would be to have the network use incomplete information as input for learning and querying, and to make the agent able to query its semantic memory for object properties given some partial input.
elements.sent-2411-221: It seems to us that this would bring the agent to draw non-monotonic inferences in the spirit of \cite{Gri16 .}
elements.sent-2411-222: It would also be useful to allow the agent to distinguish between objects and their locations, since actions can modify one, the other or both.
elements.sent-2411-223: Biological brains achieve this by using two separate pathways to process the ``what'' and the ``where'' components of observations before reunifying them, and this could be an inspiration source.
elements.sent-2411-224: Lastly, a completely different line of research would be to investigate how the agent should decide between Exploration and Exploitation modes in an open world.
elements.sent-2411-225: We present the architecture of a fully autonomous, bio-inspired cognitive agent built around a spiking neural network (SNN) implementing the agent's semantic memory.
elements.sent-2411-226: This agent explores its universe and learns concepts of objects/situations and of its own actions in a one-shot manner.
elements.sent-2411-227: Our experiments show that the agent handles new situations by appealing to previously learned general concepts and rapidly modifies its concepts to adapt to environment changes.
elements.sent-2411-228: Its main thrust is that natural agents' ability to perform well in our open and changing world relies on the fact that they store their knowledge in the form of rapidly updatable concepts with various degrees of generality.
elements.sent-2411-229: Presumably, they first form concepts about the encountered objects/situations, and then use these as elements for composing concepts of actions supporting their knowledge of their environment's action laws.
elements.sent-2411-230: At first, the agent is confined to one single room and learns by itself how to act in it.
elements.sent-2411-231: Then at some point a door opens to a new room containing some never encountered before objects and situations.
elements.sent-2411-232: To our knowledge, no existing approach addresses this problem in all its dimensions, but these are investigated in separate research fields.
elements.sent-2411-233: Continual Learning tackles the problem of lifelong knowledge acquisition \cite{WanEtAl24,Les20 .}
elements.sent-2411-234: Concept Learning has mainly been studied in view of explainability \cite{Gup2024 , mostly of classification models (e.g., \cite{Koh2020 ) but also of decision making in the context of reinforcement learning \cite{DasEtAl2023,ZabEtAl2023 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-235: In the field of Image Classification some approaches deal with the extraction of concepts from data \cite{WanEtAl2022,Gho19,Has2019 , but then these are extracted from labelled classes of images, which is unsuitable for open world autonomous agents.}
elements.sent-2411-236: The vast majority of these methods also disregard the hierarchical organization of concepts from particular to more general, and they generally do not address one-shot learning, online revision or updating of concepts.
elements.sent-2411-237: But this method does not achieve generalization nor accommodate environment changes and only considers the universally applicable actions (i.e., actions that can be executed in every logically possible state), a condition real-world actions rarely satisfy.
elements.sent-2411-238: But said instances come with their own set of ground actions which must be known beforehand, so this approach cannot be used in open worlds, where an agent needs to incrementally learn from experience.
elements.sent-2411-239: Our work could be related to model-based approaches of Reinforcement Learning (RL) \cite{Moe2023 , but it differs from them in two important aspects.}
elements.sent-2411-240: Despite their undeniable successes, RL approaches struggle to adapt to environment changes and to revise learned policies \cite{Kir2023,Far2018 .}
elements.sent-2411-241: We believe that an agent learning and updating in real time a model of the environment and using it to make its decisions would be able to quickly adapt its behavior.
elements.sent-2411-242: Second, the learning process we propose does not depend on the existence of rewards, which makes it usable in contexts where rewards are scarce.
elements.sent-2411-243: The simplicity of this environment is meant to allow the precise monitoring and testing of the agent's learning and decision making abilities (section \ref{sec: Results below), so to provide a proof of concept of our approach.}
elements.sent-2411-244: Scalability and considered developments of the approach are discussed in our last section.
elements.sent-2411-245: Neural networks doing this while relying on unsupervised learning from unlabelled data already exist (e.g., \cite{ThiEtAl18,Khe17 ), so we simply suppose that the agent's perceptual system operates as intended and provides the semantic memory with the appropriate inputs, namely, the correct and complete set of features of the agent's current location.}
elements.sent-2411-246: Semantic memory forms concepts by binding together sets of features, and stores them for further retrieval.
elements.sent-2411-247: Formally, an action is a triple made up of a depart location, a motor activity, and an outcome.
elements.sent-2411-248: The set of motor activities features the agent is able to perceive by proprioception is the set \motFeat\ = $\{$\NDeplFname, \NEDeplFname, \EDeplFname, \SEDeplFname, \SDeplFname, \SWDeplFname, \WDeplFname, \NWDeplFname, \DiagFname, \OrthFname$\}$, where the first eight are specific to each particular direction, while \DiagFname\ and \OrthFname\ are shared by all motor activities yielding diagonal/orthogonal moves.
elements.sent-2411-249: The agent can form two kinds of concepts.
elements.sent-2411-250: We take inspiration in the JAST learning rule \cite{ThoEtAl19,Tor23 , which is a simplified version of STDP where the sum of the afferent connections weights on any given neuron remains constant through learning.}
elements.sent-2411-251: However, contrary to JAST we do not use binary weights but natural numbers, and we do not freeze neurons after learning, so as to allow updating.
elements.sent-2411-252: This architecture draws on neuroanatomical studies according to which concepts are represented in the brain by hierarchically organized concept neurons, each receiving information from some lower neurons and sending reciprocal connections to these same neurons so that it can reactivate them for information retrieval \cite{Qui12,BauEtAl21,Shi10 .}

elements.sent-2411-256: The network's digital implementation can be found in the provided code.. {\textbf {O-neurons' Learning.}}
elements.sent-2411-257: Each time the agent observes its current location, information from the perceptual system is sent to the network's interface, inducing the firing of the I-neurons encoding the location's features.
elements.sent-2411-258: This favors the firing of neurons that have been inactive for a long time, which can then be re-allocated to a new concept through learning.
elements.sent-2411-259: This procedure tends to reinforce O-neurons' connections with I-neurons encoding well shared features at the expense of connections with those encoding more specific features, fostering the learning of general concepts.
elements.sent-2411-260: For the other learning neurons, the learning process is similar as in first case.
elements.sent-2411-261: Given an envisaged motor activity $m$, the decision system then sends an input to the I-neurons that encode $m$'s features.
elements.sent-2411-262: Now, among the A-neurons that receive a significant amount of input, some encode more particular action concepts than others.
elements.sent-2411-263: Obviously, we want the neurons supporting more particular concepts to take precedence over those supporting more general concepts, as they convey more adequate information.
elements.sent-2411-264: To achieve this, we first assess the generality of the object concepts (/motor activity features) encoded by O- (/ I-) neurons and then we modulate the input they send to A-neurons by applying to it a multiplying factor that depends on that assessed generality (the more particular the input concept/feature, the greater the multiplying factor).
elements.sent-2411-265: Generality is assessed by checking the weight sum of O- (/ I-) neurons' connections to A-neurons, as neurons with fewer outward connections tend to encode more particular concepts.. {\textbf {A-neurons' Learning.}}
elements.sent-2411-266: The learning process depends on the accuracy of the agent's predictions relative to the action's outcome.
elements.sent-2411-267: If these are correct (i.e., all the expected features are actually present) and complete (i.e., all the actually present features were expected), then the learning process promotes generalization by erasing and replacing inactive synapses on all three compartments, otherwise it allows a few neurons to specialize on the input by also erasing and replacing some active synapses (see Supplementary Material for details).
elements.sent-2411-268: Suppose the agent is at some depart location and observes it: information from its perceptual system triggers the firing of the I-neurons encoding the location's features, which brings a number of O-neurons to fire and send inputs to A-neurons' first compartments.
elements.sent-2411-269: Concomitantly, the information is transmitted to the decision system, which decides to make a step.
elements.sent-2411-270: The agent's choice of a motor activity depends on whether it wants to exploit its current knowledge about its environment, or to explore it to improve its knowledge.
elements.sent-2411-271: The exploration/exploitation dilemma is a well-known problem in online learning \cite{Wat89,SutBar18 , and changing environments make it even more difficult.}
elements.sent-2411-272: We therefore do not try to reach an optimal solution here, but instead we simply make the agent's decision system choose at random with equal probability between an Exploration and an Exploitation mode.
elements.sent-2411-273: This choice being made, for each motor activity $m$ out of the eight possible the decision system queries the semantic memory for the outcome of the action having the current location for initial situation and $m$ for motor activity.
elements.sent-2411-274: It then rates each of them for its suitability, by building the sets $\mathit{S}$ (for ``Suitable''), $\mathit{US}$ (``Unsuitable'') and $\mathit{UD}$ (``Undecided''):.
elements.sent-2411-275: The decision system then chooses a motor activity depending on the selected mode.
elements.sent-2411-276: In Exploration mode, the agent is willing to take risks and chooses an action with the most uncertain outcome possible: if $\mathit{UD}\neq \emptyset$ it picks one from it, otherwise it goes for one with the least $c$ in $\mathit{S}\cup \mathit{US}$.
elements.sent-2411-277: In Exploitation mode by contrast, the agent just wants to avoid KO boxes and failure as much as possible.
elements.sent-2411-278: So, if $\mathit{S}\neq\emptyset$ it chooses one with the greatest $c$, otherwise if $\mathit{UD}\neq\emptyset$ it picks one from it, and if both $\mathit{S}$ and $\mathit{UD $ are empty it chooses one with the least $c$ in $\mathit{US}$.}
elements.sent-2411-279: To test the agent's learning abilities, we placed it at location $(0,0)$ and prompted it to perform a succession of series of steps, each complete sequence of series of steps being called a trial.
elements.sent-2411-280: In a first group of tests, trials consisted in 65536 steps in total with the door kept closed all along, so that the agent had no access to the second room.
elements.sent-2411-281: First, we tested the agent's ability to learn an action over one single experience (one-shot learning).
elements.sent-2411-282: To do so, after each step we asked the agent to redo the prediction that led to the just realized action, and compared this new prediction with the action's actual outcome.
elements.sent-2411-283: Values for the second room (grey lines) show that despite never having been in this room (since we kept the door closed) the agent was able to correctly predict \OKFname\ and \KOFname\ features and to a lesser extent \FailFname\ ---and this, even though locations from the second room have different sets of features, including for some of them a new feature, \SoundFname.
elements.sent-2411-284: The poor performance at wall prediction is due to the lack of general rules of the universe observable in the first room regarding the presence of walls in adjacent boxes.

elements.sent-2411-307: As regards computing time, it takes about 12 seconds on a conventional computer for the agent to run 2048 steps while keeping track of all the test data.
elements.sent-2411-308: We made no attempt to optimize the computing time, as it seems less critical in the case of online learning of autonomous agents, which can learn while physically performing their actions.
elements.sent-2411-309: In this paper we have given a proof of concept of the architecture of a fully autonomous agent that learns action laws online and accommodates environment changes.
elements.sent-2411-310: This makes it well suited for open worlds: if a new door were to open to a third room with new objects and laws, it would learn them just as it did in the second one.
elements.sent-2411-311: In fact, the agent's ability to selectively forget its less used knowledge ensures that it will always be able to adapt to new environments by replacing old unused concepts by new useful ones.
elements.sent-2411-312: As regard scalability to larger environments, the number of neurons needed in each layer of the network (interface, O-neurons, A-neurons) grows linearly with the number of items (features, object concepts, action concepts) to encode; the number of synapses needed on each O-neuron grows linearly with the maximum number of features to be remembered for a given object/situation.
elements.sent-2411-313: However the agent's ability to form general concepts out of individual experiences and to use them to make decisions makes it unnecessary to encode each individual object with all its features and each performed action, so these are upper bounds.
elements.sent-2411-314: Further work remains to be done to allow the agent to live in more complex and realistic environments.
elements.sent-2411-315: A first direction of work would be to endow the agent with planning abilities.
elements.sent-2411-316: To do so, one would need a notion of applicable action law.
elements.sent-2411-317: It seems that an action law represented by an action concept \cpt{x,\,y,\,z could be deemed applicable in a situation $s$ if $s$ satisfies all the features in \cptFeat{x and \cptFeat{z $\neq$ \FailFname.}}}
elements.sent-2411-318: To live in open worlds the agent would need to build its own set of possible situations (states) online.
elements.sent-2411-319: It appears that the set of its object concepts could be used for this.
elements.sent-2411-320: The decision system should be augmented so as to handle goals and a cost function.
elements.sent-2411-321: Another beneficial improvement would be to make the agent able to use incomplete information as input for learning and querying, as real world agents' observations are rarely complete.
elements.sent-2411-322: We could then make the agent able to query its semantic memory for object properties given some partial input.
elements.sent-2411-323: It would also be useful to implement negation in the network, to allow the agent to represent the fact that a given object does not have a given feature.
elements.sent-2411-324: Neural inhibition could probably be used for this, but the appropriate learning rules remain to be found.
elements.sent-2411-325: It seems to us that taken together these two improvements would bring the agent to draw non-monotonic inferences in the spirit of \cite{Gri16 .}
elements.sent-2411-326: It would also be useful to allow the agent to distinguish between objects and their locations, as actions can modify one, the other or both.
elements.sent-2411-327: Biological brains process the ``what'' and the ``where'' components of observations in two separate pathways before reunifying them, and this could be an inspiration source.
elements.sent-2411-328: A last line of research would be to investigate how an agent should decide between Exploration and Exploitation modes in an open world.. \appendixpage.

elements.sent-2411-331: The lower CC prediction scores around 128-2048 steps can be explained as follows.
elements.sent-2411-332: At start up, the network has a lot of unused neurons at its disposal and simply learns the particular concepts with all their features, so specific features are well learned.
elements.sent-2411-333: But as the network accumulates knowledge, unused neurons get scarce and the network recruits less used neurons for learning.
elements.sent-2411-334: A trade-off is made between learning the current observation’s specific features and not forgetting the common features already encoded by the neurons, which makes specific features more difficult to learn.
elements.sent-2411-335: Yet after repeated encounters the specific features are finally learned, so the score improves again.

elements.sent-2411-431: They embody the agent's knowledge of its universe's action laws.
elements.sent-2411-432: The ability of a cognitive agent to act adequately in a given environment depends on its ability to predict how performing a given action will affect its current situation; in other words, it depends on the agent's knowledge of its environment's action laws.
elements.sent-2411-433: How artificial agents can acquire these laws and how these should be updated if their environment changes has proved a difficult question.
elements.sent-2411-434: It should also allow for rapid modification of the learned action laws to accommodate environment changes.
elements.sent-2411-435: The aim of the present paper is to show how this could be done.
elements.sent-2411-436: Its main hypothesis is that natural agents' ability to perform well in our open and changing world relies on the fact that they store their knowledge in the form of rapidly updatable concepts with various degrees of generality.
elements.sent-2411-437: Presumably, they first form concepts about the encountered objects/situations, and then use these as elements for composing concepts of actions.
elements.sent-2411-438: The latter constitutes their knowledge of their environment's action laws.
elements.sent-2411-439: Another important hypothesis is that natural agents' endless ability to learn new concepts despite having finite brains relies on some efficacious management of forgetting.
elements.sent-2411-440: Most likely, they selectively forget their less important knowledge (such as, e.g., details and unused memories) and reallocate the corresponding neurons to encode new useful knowledge.
elements.sent-2411-441: We suggest that artificial agents could take inspiration from these strategies and use some artificial neural network to learn and store concepts, and query this network to make predictions about the outcome of envisaged actions.
elements.sent-2411-442: To test this idea, we here build an artificial hybrid agent with an SNN at its core.
elements.sent-2411-443: This agent lives in a very simple virtual world, composed of rooms which may be, or not, accessible (hence, knowable) to it.
elements.sent-2411-444: Our experiments show that having learned these laws in the first room allows the agent to act by and large properly in the second one, as soon as it enters it.
elements.sent-2411-445: They also show that the agent is able to learn new laws holding in the second room without catastrophic loss of previous knowledge.
elements.sent-2411-446: Finally, some changes are introduced in the second room, rendering some of the previously learned rules obsolete while some new rules become true.
elements.sent-2411-447: Again, experiments show that the agent quickly updates its knowledge to account for these changes.

elements.sent-2411-451: The research problem addressed in this work is autonomous online learning, generalization and updating of concepts and action laws in an open universe.
elements.sent-2411-452: The intended application is to use the learned concepts in reasoning and planning for autonomous robots.
elements.sent-2411-453: Continual Learning tackles the problem of lifelong knowledge acquisition \citep{WanEtAl24,Les20 .}
elements.sent-2411-454: Its main challenge is to avoid catastrophic loss of previous knowledge when acquiring new knowledge; a secondary research axis is one-shot/few-shots learning, i.e., the ability to learn online from one or few examples \citep{WanEtAl20 .}
elements.sent-2411-455: However, current approaches mostly consider the learning of tasks (mostly image classification/recognition tasks, but also some more complex tasks such as playing games \citep{KirEtAl17 ), not of concepts nor action laws.}
elements.sent-2411-456: Concept Learning has mainly been studied in view of explainability \citep{Gup2024 , mostly of classification models (e.g., \citep{Koh2020 ) but also of decision making in the context of reinforcement learning \citep{DasEtAl2023,ZabEtAl2023 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-457: In the field of Image Classification some approaches deal with the extraction of concepts from data \citep{WanEtAl2022,Gho19,Has2019 , but then these are extracted from labelled classes of images, which again is unsuitable for open world autonomous agents.}
elements.sent-2411-458: The vast majority of these methods also disregards the hierarchical organization of concepts from particular to more general, and they generally do not address one-shot learning, online revision or updating of concepts.
elements.sent-2411-459: Action Laws Learning has been studied from various perspectives.
elements.sent-2411-460: In Dynamic Epistemic Logic, \citep{Bol18 proposed a method to learn an action model through successive observations of transitions between states.}
elements.sent-2411-461: But this method does not achieve generalization nor accommodate environment changes and only considers universally applicable actions (i.e., actions that can be executed in every logically possible state), a condition real-world actions rarely satisfy.
elements.sent-2411-462: In the field of Planning, \citep{BonEtAl2019 showed how to learn abstract actions from a few carefully chosen instances of some general planning problem.}
elements.sent-2411-463: Reinforcement Learning (RL) is concerned with agents learning by experience how to achieve a goal in an optimal manner.
elements.sent-2411-464: A number of variants have been developed, which all have in common that the agent learns through trials and errors by means of a reward system, and that what is finally learned is a policy, i.e., a function which takes a given state of the agent as an argument and returns an action (or a set of actions) to be taken.
elements.sent-2411-465: RL approaches have proved very successful in a wide range of domains, and for this reason they are quite popular.
elements.sent-2411-466: However, they struggle to adapt to environment changes and to revise the learned policies \citep{Kir2023,Far2018 .}
elements.sent-2411-467: This could be related with the fact that in natural agents, learning policies is rather a matter of procedural memory (``memory of procedures''), a memory system that typically requires a large number of learning trials \citep{Tul85, KnoEtAl17 .}
elements.sent-2411-468: By contrast, the semantic memory, which stores facts about the world in the form of concepts, is a fast learning system that can learn or update a concept over one single experience.
elements.sent-2411-469: It thus seems that an artificial agent able to learn and update in real time a conceptual model of its environment and to use it to make its decisions would be able to quickly adapt to environment changes.
elements.sent-2411-470: RL approaches also typically encounter difficulties in contexts where rewards are scarce or deceptive \citep{Har2019,Oca2023 , which is the case of most real world contexts.}
elements.sent-2411-471: Although some solutions have been proposed (see notably \citep{Eco2021 ), dealing with sparse rewards is still an active field of research in RL.}
elements.sent-2411-472: But since concept learning does not depend on rewards, an agent able to learn concepts and to use them to make its decisions would be immune to the problem.

elements.sent-2411-473: In the field of robotics, semantic information has long been recognized as instrumental in autonomous robots' decision making \citep{Pra2020 , but there have been very few attempts to make autonomous robots capable of learning semantic knowledge from experience.}
elements.sent-2411-474: The most prominent are probably \citep{WanEtAl2016 and \citep{Nas2019 , which both use fusion ART neural networks to encode concepts.}}
elements.sent-2411-475: However these neural networks rely on the extension of their architecture to learn new knowledge (i.e., new neurons are created to encode new concepts), which is unsuitable for agents with finite memory resources living in an open world.
elements.sent-2411-476: In open worlds, the finiteness of autonomous agents’ memory makes forgetting unavoidable.
elements.sent-2411-477: For this reason, memorizing the whole of the experienced objects and events cannot be taken as an ultimate goal or criterion for such agents.
elements.sent-2411-478: Instead, forgetting should be accepted as a necessary counterpart of learning, and managed so as to preserve the agent's performance as much as possible.
elements.sent-2411-479: That is, what should be avoided is catastrophic forgetting.
elements.sent-2411-480: The most natural way to do this is by ensuring that the knowledge lost through learning is always the agent's less useful knowledge at the time of the learning.
elements.sent-2411-481: How to characterize ``less useful knowledge'' and how to identify the neurons supporting it in order to preferentially select them for encoding new knowledge is a key issue to be addressed in the sequel.
elements.sent-2411-482: The present paper aims at providing a proof of concept of a cognitive agent satisfying the above requirements.
elements.sent-2411-483: As a first step, it focuses on the learning of concepts and only uses them in some basic decision making to demonstrate the agent's learning abilities.
elements.sent-2411-484: However the agent is designed so as to allow the retrieval of the learned concepts and action laws, which could then be encoded in symbolic format and used in more complex decision making involving explicit reasoning and action planning.
elements.sent-2411-485: The implementation of such complex reasoning abilities in the agent is left to future work.
elements.sent-2411-486: In the absence of studies investigating the considered research problem in all its dimensions, comparing the proposed setup with existing approaches seems inappropriate.
elements.sent-2411-487: Indeed, it would only take into account some of the dimensions of the problem and fail to evaluate the proposal relative to its goal.
elements.sent-2411-488: Therefore, a suitable set of experiments was devised (see Section \ref{sec: Results ).}
