elements.section-2411-014: Implementing the agent's semantic memory in the neural network
elements.sent-2411-522: Spiking Neural Networks (SNNs) are well suited for autonomous learning in open worlds, as they allow for Spike Time Dependent Plasticity (STDP), a family of biologically plausible learning rules which can achieve unsupervised online learning from unlabelled data \citep{ThiEtAl18 .}
elements.sent-2411-523: They are also known for being energy-efficient, which is an interesting property for autonomous robots.
elements.sent-2411-524: The neural network implementing the agent's semantic memory is inspired from the JAST learning rule \citep{ThoEtAl19,Tor23 , which is a simplified version of STDP where the sum of the afferent connection weights on any given neuron remains constant through learning.}
elements.sent-2411-525: However, contrary to what is the case in JAST here the weights are not binary but are natural numbers, and neurons are not frozen after learning so as to allow updating.
elements.sent-2411-526: This architecture draws on neuroanatomical studies according to which concepts are represented in the brain by hierarchically organized concept neurons, each receiving information from some lower neurons and sending reciprocal connections to these same neurons so that it can reactivate them for information retrieval \citep{Qui12,BauEtAl21,Shi10 .}
elements.sent-2411-527: For simplicity these reciprocal connections are not modeled as such, but instead information is allowed to flow in both directions along the same connections: from interface neurons to O-neurons and from there to A-neurons for learning and querying, and the other way round for retrieving information.
elements.sent-2411-528: A key point is that interface neurons are both input and output neurons, depending on the phase of the computation.
elements.sent-2411-530: The first compartment receives connections from O-neurons, the second from motor activities I-neurons, and the third from O-neurons and the Failure neuron.
elements.sent-2411-531: Inputs received at each compartment are unable to trigger a spike by themselves, but the first and second compartments respectively make their next compartment ready to receive and transmit inputs for a certain amount of time.
elements.sent-2411-532: The use of compartment neurons to learn sequences of inputs was suggested in \citep{Cui16,HawEtAl16 .}
elements.sent-2411-533: For readability, parameter settings are provided in the Appendices.
elements.sent-2411-534: We note \O\ the set of O-neurons and \A\ the set of A-neurons.
elements.sent-2411-535: For any neurons $l$ from \L\ and $o$ from \O, \w{l, o denotes the weight of the connection between $l$ and $o$.}
elements.sent-2411-536: Similarly, for any compartment $c$ from $\{1, 2, 3\}$, any neuron $n$ from $\O \cup \M \cup \F$ and any A-neuron $a$, \wComp{c {n, a denotes the weight of $n$'s connection on $a$'s $c^{th}$ compartment.}}
elements.sent-2411-537: Synapses between any two neurons always have a weight of $1$, but an input neuron may have multiple synapses onto a same output neuron, so the weight of a connection between two neurons is always a positive integer (or 0, if there is no connection).
elements.sent-2411-538: Connection weights are initialized at random, in such a way that the sum of weights on any O-neuron is equal to some fixed integer \WSO, and the sum of weights on any compartment of any A-neuron is equal to some fixed integer \WSA.
elements.sent-2411-539: Connexions are stored in separate arrays \cnxLO, \cnxOAi, \cnxMAii\ and \cnxOAiii, where \cnxLO\ reads ``connections from \L\ to \O'', and similarly for the others.
elements.sent-2411-540: For each O-neuron $o$ a fixed spike threshold \STO$(o)$ is set at random to a value comprised between some minimal and maximal values.
elements.sent-2411-541: This spike threshold governs forward spiking, that is, cases where the input originates from \L-neurons.
elements.sent-2411-542: Differentiated spike thresholds help the neurons to encode concepts with different degrees of generality, as neurons with lower spike thresholds tend to spike more easily to inputs that do not fit perfectly their connections, which brings them to learn more general concepts.
elements.sent-2411-543: O-neurons also have a backward spike threshold \backwardSTO$(o)$, which governs their spiking when the input originates from A-neurons.
elements.sent-2411-544: To ease information retrieval, \backwardSTO$(o)$ is set to \STO$(o)$/3.
elements.sent-2411-545: L-neurons all have the same spike threshold \STL, and the Failure neuron has its own spike threshold.
elements.sent-2411-546: Spike thresholds for A-neurons are dynamically adjusted, in a way that is described below.. {\textbf {O-neurons.}}
elements.sent-2411-547: Given a set \inputLO\ of L-neurons firing in response to an input from either the perceptual system (if the agent is observing its current location) or the decision system (if the agent is querying its semantic memory), we note \firedO\ the set of O-neurons firing in response to \inputLO.
elements.sent-2411-549: First, for any neuron $o$ in \O\ the sum \inputSumO$(o)$ of input received by $o$ is computed.
elements.sent-2411-551: The difference \overT$(o)$ with $o$'s spike threshold is then calculated (that is, \overT$(o) = $ \inputSumO$(o) - $\STO$(o)$).
elements.sent-2411-552: Neurons $o$ for which \overT$(o)$ is positive (i.e., which have reached their spike threshold) then fire in turn, starting by those with the highest \overT$(o)$, until either no O-neuron with positive \overT\ value remains or the number of firing O-neurons reaches some target number \TnbFiredO.
elements.sent-2411-553: This target number is a fixed parameter of the network that helps to keep the number of firing O-neurons under control.
elements.sent-2411-554: For any O-neuron $o$ the number of steps performed by the agent since $o$'s last spiking is noted \lastSpikedO$(o)$.
elements.sent-2411-555: It is reset to 0 each time the neuron spikes after learning.
elements.sent-2411-556: The O-neurons firing in response to the observation input \inputLO\ are selected for learning.
elements.sent-2411-557: That is, we set \learningO\ $=$ \firedO, where \learningO\ is the set of O-neurons selected for learning.
elements.sent-2411-558: If there are enough learning neurons, that is, if $\mid$\learningO$\mid$ reaches some target number \TnbQueryO\ (with \TnbQueryO\ a fixed parameter of the network such that \TnbQueryO\ $<$ \TnbFiredO), the network directly proceeds to learn.
elements.sent-2411-559: Otherwise it first looks for additional neurons by boosting the input received by O-neurons.
elements.sent-2411-560: In practice, for any non-firing O-neuron we define \boostedInputSumO$(o) = $ \inputSumO$(o) * ($\lastSpikedO$(o)+ b)/b$, with $b \in \mathbb{N}$ a fixed parameter.
elements.sent-2411-561: Neurons with the highest boosted input sum values are then successively added to \learningO\ until $\mid$\learningO$\mid$ $\geq$ \TnbQueryO.
elements.sent-2411-562: In this manner, O-neurons that have not been used for a long time are preferentially recruited for learning new information.
elements.sent-2411-563: Of course, some of the information encoded by these neurons will be lost in the next learning process, bringing the agent to forget some of its previous knowledge.
elements.sent-2411-564: But this will only affect the agent's least used knowledge, as these neurons are the least used at the time of the learning.
elements.sent-2411-565: The learning of O-neurons essentially consists in replacing their connections from non-firing L-neurons with connections from firing L-neurons (namely, the neurons from \inputLO).
elements.sent-2411-566: To decide which neuron from \inputLO\ will establish new synapses with a given O-neuron, a probability distribution \probaNewSynapsesLO\ is defined.
elements.sent-2411-567: For any neuron $l$ in \inputLO, \probaNewSynapsesLO$(l)$ represents $l$'s propensity to grow new synapses to O-neurons at time point $t$.
elements.sent-2411-568: It accounts for the fact that neurons cannot indefinitely grow new outgoing synapses, hence that a neuron having already a lot of them should be less prone to establish new connexions than a neuron having fewer of them.
elements.sent-2411-569: It ensures that L-neurons encoding rarely observed features (such as, e.g., boxes' names) still manage to have connections with at least a few O-neurons.
elements.sent-2411-570: The learning process then depends on the accuracy of the agent's knowledge relative to the current situation.
elements.sent-2411-571: To assess it, O-neurons from \firedO\ send a backward input to L-neurons, and the resulting set \firedL\ of firing L-neurons is compared with the initial input \inputLO.
elements.sent-2411-572: This corresponds to the agent comparing what it observes (represented by \inputLO) with what it would have expected based on its current knowledge (represented by \firedL).
elements.sent-2411-573: If \inputLO\ $\subseteq$ \firedL, that is, if all the observed features can be retrieved from the firing O-neurons, then for any neurons $o$ in \learningO\ and $l$ in \L$\setminus$\inputLO, the connection weight \w{l, o is reset to 0 (i.e., all of $o$'s inactive synapses are deleted).}
elements.sent-2411-574: They are then replaced with new synapses from neurons from \inputLO\ following the above-mentioned probability distribution, in such a way that $\sum w_{l, o}$ for $l \in$ \L\ remains equal to \WSO.
elements.sent-2411-575: This procedure tends to reinforce O-neurons' connections with L-neurons encoding well-shared features at the expense of connexions with L-neurons encoding more specific features, and is the driving force for the formation of general object concepts.
elements.sent-2411-576: If, on the contrary, \inputLO\ $\not \subseteq$ \firedL, then we first check if $\mid$\learningO$\mid$ reaches \TnbFiredO.
elements.sent-2411-577: If not, the network looks for one more neuron to add to \learningO, using the same method as above except that only one neuron is added.
elements.sent-2411-578: The learning process is then similar as before, except that for one O-neuron $o$ with maximal \lastSpikedO$(o)$, all synapses whether active or inactive are replaced.
elements.sent-2411-579: Doing so favors the establishment of connections with all the neurons from \inputLO, so that this particular neuron tends to learn the particular situation with all its features.
elements.sent-2411-580: At any point in time, we respectively note \inputOAi, \inputMAii\ and \inputOAiii\ the sets of neurons from \O, \M\ and \O\ $\cup$ \F\ that send inputs to A-neurons' first, second and third compartments.
elements.sent-2411-581: These inputs may occur in two different situations: when the decision system queries the neural network for the expected outcome of an envisaged action, or when the neural network learns about a given action.
elements.sent-2411-582: To query the neural network, the decision system first sends an input to the L-neurons that encode the features of the initial situation, bringing a set \inputLO\ of L-neurons to fire.
elements.sent-2411-583: This in turn triggers the firing of a set \firedO\ of O-neurons, following the above-described process.
elements.sent-2411-584: Given an envisaged motor activity \Move, the decision system then sends an input to the M-neurons that encode \Move's features.
elements.sent-2411-585: These fire, sending an input \inputMAii\ to A-neurons' second compartment.
elements.sent-2411-586: Querying the network amounts to bringing some A-neurons to spike in response to this incomplete input and send backward input to O-neurons, so that these spike and send backward input to interface L-neurons.
elements.sent-2411-587: The resulting firing of interface L-neurons is the network's answer to the query.
elements.sent-2411-588: Now, among the A-neurons that receive some input from O- and M- neurons, some support the representation of general action concepts (such as, e.g., \cpt{\cpt{OK ,\,\cpt{NE ,\,\cpt{OK ), while others support the representation of more particular concepts (such as, say, \cpt{\cpt{OK, NorthWall ,\,\cpt{NE ,\,\cpt{Failure ).}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-589: Obviously, it is desirable that the neurons supporting the more particular concepts take precedence over those supporting more general concepts, as more particular concepts convey more adequate information.
elements.sent-2411-590: To achieve this, the input sent to A-neurons is modulated by applying a multiplying factor to the neurons' connections weights.
elements.sent-2411-591: More precisely, for any neuron $o$ in \inputOAi, this multiplying factor is a decreasing function of $\sum w^1_{o, a}$ for $a \in$ \A, and similarly for \inputMAii.
elements.sent-2411-592: The idea behind this is that neurons with fewer output connections tend to support more particular concepts than neurons with more output connections, hence that the A-neurons to which they connect also tend encode more particular concepts.
elements.sent-2411-593: Modulated input sums are computed separately for each compartment of each A-neuron $a$, in a way similar to that used for O-neurons.
elements.sent-2411-594: If the input sum received by $a$ on its first compartment is above some noise threshold \noiseA, then $a$'s second compartment is able to transmit its input to $a$.
elements.sent-2411-595: The total input received by $a$ is then computed as the sum of the two inputs.
elements.sent-2411-597: Due to input modulation, the input sum received by a given A-neuron $a$ indicates both the accuracy of the action concept encoded by $a$ relative to the input pair (\inputOAi, \inputMAii), and the concept's degree of generality: the more adequate and particular the encoded concept, the higher this input sum.
elements.sent-2411-598: The set \firedA\ of firing A-neurons is obtained by first setting A-neurons' spike threshold to the highest input sum value (so only A-neurons with the highest input sum fire), and then repeatedly lowering it to the next highest value until the number of firing A-neurons reaches a target number \TnbQueryA.
elements.sent-2411-599: Each time the spike threshold is lowered all the A-neurons with input sums above the threshold spike again, so A-neurons with higher inputs sums spike repeatedly.
elements.sent-2411-600: Each time an A-neuron spikes, it sends a backward input to O- and Failure neurons through its third compartment's connections.
elements.sent-2411-601: Backward input sums are computed incrementally as A-neurons fire, bringing O- and Failure neurons to fire as soon as their spike threshold is reached.
elements.sent-2411-602: If some O-neuron fires first, it inhibits the Failure neuron and prevents it from further firing.
elements.sent-2411-603: Similarly, if Failure neuron spikes first, it inhibits all the O-neurons and prevents them from further firing.
elements.sent-2411-604: Firing O-neurons in turn send backward inputs to L-neurons and the input sum received by L-neurons is computed incrementally.
elements.sent-2411-605: L-neurons spike as soon as they reach their threshold, and their firing inhibits the L-neurons that encode incompatible features (for example, the firing of the L-neuron encoding the feature \OKFname\ prevents the L-neuron encoding the feature \KOFname\ from further firing).
elements.sent-2411-606: The value of A-neurons' spike threshold at the moment a given neuron $n \in $ \L\ $\cup$ \F spikes measures the relevance of the A-neurons involved in $n$'s spiking relative to the input pair (\inputOAi, \inputMAii).
elements.sent-2411-607: It can therefore be used by the agent to assess the reliability of its prediction.
elements.sent-2411-608: In other words, it indicates the degree of confidence the agent has in its prediction of the feature encoded by $n$: the higher this value, the higher the confidence.
elements.sent-2411-609: So, formally, the querying process returns a prediction set $P_\mathit{Move = \{(f_1,$ \conf$_1), ...}}
elements.sent-2411-610: In this case, \inputOAi\ is the set \firedO\ resulting from the agent observing its depart location, \inputMAii\ is the set of M-neurons firing by proprioception as the agent performs the motor activity, and \inputOAiii\ is the set \firedO\ resulting from the agent observing its arrival location.
elements.sent-2411-611: For any neuron $a$ in \A, \inputSumAiAii$(a)$ is sum of the inputs received by $a$ on its first and second compartments.
elements.sent-2411-612: That is, \inputSumAiAii$(a) = \sum w^1_{o, a}$ for $o \in$ \inputOAi$ +(\sum w^2_{m, a}$ for $m \in$ \inputMAii$)$.
elements.sent-2411-613: If \inputSumAiAii$(a)$ reaches a certain threshold \learnAT\ and $a$ fired in the querying process by which the agent predicted the outcome of the current action (see Section \ref{sec: agent's functioning below), then $a$ is selected for learning.}
elements.sent-2411-614: The set of such A-neurons is denoted \learningA.
elements.sent-2411-615: If $\mid$\learningA $\mid$ reaches a target number \TnbQueryA\ the network directly proceeds to learn.
elements.sent-2411-616: Otherwise, additional neurons are selected by boosting the input sums received by A-neurons in a way similar to the one previously described for O-neurons.
elements.sent-2411-617: For each A-neuron $a$, the number of steps performed by the agent since $a$'s last spiking is noted \lastSpikedA$(a)$.
elements.sent-2411-618: It is reset to 0 each time the neuron spikes after learning (i.e., spikes occurring during the querying process are discounted).
elements.sent-2411-619: For each input set $i$ in $\{$\inputOAi, \inputMAii, \inputOAiii$\}$, a probability distribution \probaNewSynapsesA$_i$ is computed on $i$ that models the propensity of each neuron $n$ from $i$ to grow new synapses to A-neurons' corresponding compartments.
elements.sent-2411-620: This probability is a decreasing function of $\sum w^{c _{n, a}$ for $a \in$ \A, where $c \in \{1, 2, 3\}$ depends on the input set.}
elements.sent-2411-621: For each A-neuron $a$ in \learningA, a learning rate \LR$(a)$ is computed.
elements.sent-2411-622: This learning rate is an integer comprised between $1$ and \WSA\ (Remember that \WSA\ is the sum of connections weights on any A-neuron compartment).
elements.sent-2411-623: The learning process then depends on the accuracy of the agent's expectations relative to the action's outcome.
elements.sent-2411-624: These expectations were obtained by querying the network before choosing to perform the action.
elements.sent-2411-625: If these expectations were both correct (i.e., all expected features are actually present) and complete (i.e., all actually present features were expected), then for any A-neuron $a$ in \learningA, the learning process deletes all of $a$'s inactive synapses in the first two compartments, but only some of them in the third one.
elements.sent-2411-626: More precisely, if $n$ is the number of inactive synapses on $a$'s third compartment, the number of inactive synapses to delete is $min($\LR$(a), n)$.
elements.sent-2411-627: Then, in all three compartments new synapses replacing the deleted ones are chosen at random according to \probaNewSynapsesA$_i$ (where $i$ is the input set corresponding to the considered compartment), in such a way that the sum of connection weights (i.e., the number of synapses) on the compartment remains equal to \WSA.
elements.sent-2411-628: If the agent's expectations were incorrect, then for any $a$ in \learningA\ the learning process not only replaces all inactive synapses on $a$'s first two compartments, but also some active ones.
elements.sent-2411-629: Here the number of synapses to be replaced is $max($\LR$(a), n)$, where $n$ is the number of inactive synapses, and the deleted active synapses are chosen at random.
elements.sent-2411-630: As previously with O-neurons, replacing some active synapses in addition to the inactive ones allows the considered compartments to get connections from more neurons from the input set, and so to ``re-specialize'' relative to the input.
elements.sent-2411-631: Making the first two compartments more specialized to, respectively, the initial situation and the motor activity helps the neuron to fire only in response to this precise action, hence to retain more accurate information about its outcome.
elements.sent-2411-632: If the agent's expectations were incomplete, then for any A-neuron $a$ in \learningA\ the number of replaced synapses is $max($\LR$(a), n)$ in all three compartments.
elements.sent-2411-633: Suppose the agent is at some initial location and observes it: information from its perceptual system triggers the firing of the L-neurons encoding the location's features, which brings a number of O-neurons to fire and send an input \inputOAi\ to A-neurons' first compartment.
elements.sent-2411-634: Concomitantly, the information is transmitted to the agent's decision system, which decides to make a step.
elements.sent-2411-635: The exploration/exploitation dilemma is a well-known problem in online learning \citep{Wat89,SutBar18 , and evolving environments make it even more difficult.}
elements.sent-2411-636: Therefore, we do not try to reach an optimal solution here, but instead we simply make the agent's decision system choose at random with equal probability between an Exploration and an Exploitation mode.
elements.sent-2411-637: This choice being made, for each motor activity \Move\ out of the eight possible ones the decision system queries the semantic memory for the outcome of the action having the current location as initial situation and \Move\ as motor activity.
elements.sent-2411-638: In Exploration mode, the agent is willing to take risks and chooses an action with the most uncertain outcome possible so to increase its knowledge: if $\mathit{UD}\neq \emptyset$ it picks one from $\mathit{UD}$, otherwise it goes for one with the least \conf\ in $\mathit{S}\cup \mathit{US}$.

elements.sent-2411-697: In this paper a proof of concept of the architecture of a fully autonomous agent learning action laws online and accommodating environment changes was provided.
elements.sent-2411-698: This makes it well suited for open worlds: if a new door were to open to a third room with new objects and laws, it would learn them just as it did in the second room.
elements.sent-2411-699: In fact, the agent's ability to selectively forget its least used knowledge ensures that it will always be able to adapt to new environments by replacing old unused concepts by new useful ones.
elements.sent-2411-700: As regards scalability to larger environments, the number of neurons needed in each layer of the network (interface, O-neurons, A-neurons) grows linearly with the number of items (features, object concepts, action concepts) to encode; the number of synapses needed on each O-neuron grows linearly with the maximum number of features to be learned for a given object/situation.
elements.sent-2411-701: However, it is important to observe that these are upper bounds: the agent's ability to form general concepts out of individual experiences and to use them to make decisions makes it unnecessary to encode each individual object with all its features and each performed action.
elements.sent-2411-702: Future developments of the architecture include endowing the agent with planning abilities.
elements.sent-2411-703: To do so, a notion of applicable action law should be defined.
elements.sent-2411-704: In a first approach, an action law represented by an action concept \cpt{x,\,y,\,z could be deemed applicable in a situation $s$ if $s$ satisfies all the features in \cptFeat{x and \cptFeat{z $\neq$ \cpt{Failure .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-705: The agent would also need to build its own set of possible situations (states) online.
elements.sent-2411-706: The decision system should also be augmented to represent goals and embed a cost function and a planning algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-707: However further work remains to be done to allow the agent to live in more realistic environments.
elements.sent-2411-708: Notably, it would be necessary to make the agent able to use incomplete information as input for learning and querying, as real world agents' observations are rarely complete.
elements.sent-2411-709: It would then be interesting to make the agent able to query its semantic memory for object properties given some partial input.
elements.sent-2411-710: It seems that taken together these two improvements would bring the agent to draw non-monotonic inferences in the spirit of \citep{Gri16 .}
elements.sent-2411-711: It would also be suitable to make the agent able to distinguish between objects and their locations, as actions can modify one, the other, or both.
elements.sent-2411-712: A further line of research would be to investigate how an agent should decide between Exploration and Exploitation modes in an open world.
elements.sent-2411-713: Intuitively, it seems that the choice between these two operating modes should depend on the agent's estimation of the risk associated with an explorative behavior, and that the agent should refrain from entering in exploration mode in situations where the assessed risk is high, while allowing itself more exploration in situations where it is low.
elements.sent-2411-714: Yet a correct formalization of this idea remains to be found.

elements.sent-2411-719: The ability of a cognitive agent to act adequately in a given environment depends on its ability to predict how performing a given action will affect its current situation; that is, it depends on the agent's knowledge of the laws of the universe that determine the effect of its possible actions on its environment (hereafter the action laws of the universe).
elements.sent-2411-720: Finally, it should address the fact that however big its memory resources may be they will always be finite, while the number of distinct objects and situations it may encounter in an open world is unbounded.
elements.sent-2411-721: The aim of the present paper is to show how such a learning (autonomous, online, achieving generalization and rapid updating while being adapted to open universes), could be done.
elements.sent-2411-722: Solving this problem would be useful in applications such as mobile autonomous robots (e.g. service robots) living in open worlds.
elements.sent-2411-723: The proposed approach's main hypothesis is that natural agents' ability to perform well in our open and changing world largely relies on the fact that they store their knowledge in the form of rapidly updatable concepts with various degrees of generality.
elements.sent-2411-724: Presumably, natural agents first form concepts about the encountered objects/situations, and then use these as elements for composing more complex concepts, notably concepts of action.
elements.sent-2411-725: Concepts are a very compact way to store information, as more general concepts can apply to a whole range of distinct cases while being usable in new similar situations.
elements.sent-2411-726: They also allow for efficient updating, as the adjustment of a single concept modifies all the inferences and further decisions that can be made on its basis.
elements.sent-2411-727: Another important hypothesis is that natural agents' endless ability to learn new concepts despite having finite brains relies on some efficacious management of forgetting.Forgetting is inevitable for an agent with finite memory living in an open world; but it needs to avoid catastrophic forgetting, that is, some kind of forgetting that would severely affect its ability to act judiciously in its current world.
elements.sent-2411-728: Most likely, natural agents avoid catastrophic forgetting by selectively forgetting their less important knowledge (such as, e.g., details and old, unused memories) and reallocating the corresponding neurons to encode new useful knowledge.
elements.sent-2411-729: To test this idea, we here build an artificial agent with an SNN at its core.

elements.sent-2411-733: The research problem addressed in this work is autonomous online learning, updating and generalization of concepts and action laws in an open universe.
elements.sent-2411-734: Its main challenge is to avoid catastrophic loss of previous knowledge when acquiring new knowledge; a secondary research axis is one-shot/few-shots learning, which is the ability to learn online from one or few examples \cite{WanEtAl20 .}
elements.sent-2411-735: Furthermore, most of them rely on learning algorithms that use labelled training data (mainly some supervised learning algorithms), which makes them unsuitable for open world autonomous agents.
elements.sent-2411-736: In the field of Image Classification some approaches deal with the extraction of concepts from data \cite{WanEtAl2022,Gho19,Has2019 , but then these are extracted from labelled classes of images, which again is unsuitable for open world autonomous agents.}
elements.sent-2411-737: But this method does not address generalization nor environment changes and only considers universally applicable actions (i.e., actions that can be executed in every logically possible state), a condition real-world actions rarely satisfy.
elements.sent-2411-738: However, they struggle to adapt to environment changes and to revise the learned policies \cite{Kir2023,Far2018 .}
elements.sent-2411-739: This could be related with the fact that in natural agents, learning policies is rather a matter of procedural memory (``memory of procedures''), a memory system that typically requires a large number of learning trials \cite{Tul85, KnoEtAl17 .}
elements.sent-2411-740: It thus seems that an artificial agent that would be able to learn and update in real time a conceptual model of its environment and to use it to make its decisions would be able to quickly adapt to environment changes.
elements.sent-2411-741: RL approaches also typically encounter difficulties in contexts where rewards are scarce or deceptive \cite{Har2019,Oca2023 , which is the case of most real world contexts.}
elements.sent-2411-742: Although some solutions have been proposed (see notably \cite{Eco2021 ), dealing with sparse rewards is still an active field of research in RL.}
elements.sent-2411-743: In the field of robotics, semantic information has long been recognized as instrumental in autonomous robots' decision making \cite{Pra2020 , but there have been very few attempts to make robots capable of learning semantic knowledge from experience.}
elements.sent-2411-744: The most prominent are probably \cite{WanEtAl2016 and \cite{Nas2019 , which both use fusion ART (for Adaptive Resonance Theory) neural networks to encode concepts.}}
elements.sent-2411-745: However these neural networks rely on the extension of their architecture to learn new knowledge (i.e., new neurons are created to encode new concepts), which is unsuitable for autonomous agents with finite memory resources living in an open world.
elements.sent-2411-746: Agents living in complex open environments (such as the real world) can be confronted with a huge amount of information in the course of their life.
elements.sent-2411-747: Retaining the totality of that information and further processing it to use it does not seem a practical option, as this would be extremely costly in terms of memory, computational power and energy consumption.
elements.sent-2411-748: This is especially true in the case of agents such as autonomous mobile robots, for which frugality regarding these same resources can be critical.
elements.sent-2411-749: For these agents, memorizing the whole of the experienced objects and events cannot be taken as a reasonable goal or criterion.
elements.sent-2411-750: Rather, forgetting appears as the unavoidable counterpart of continual learning, and the question is how to manage it so as to preserve the agent's performance as much as possible.
elements.sent-2411-751: The most natural way to do this is by ensuring that the previous knowledge that is lost through new learning is the agent's less useful knowledge at the time of the learning.
elements.sent-2411-752: How to characterize ``less useful knowledge'' and how to preferentially select it for deletion is a key issue to address to efficiently manage forgetting.
elements.sent-2411-753: In turn, such an efficient management of forgetting appears as a necessary condition for making lifelong learning autonomous agents in complex open environments possible.
elements.sent-2411-754: Indeed, this would only take into account some of the dimensions of the problem and fail to evaluate the proposal relative to its goal.
elements.sent-2411-755: It should also be remarked that the standard metrics commonly used to assess neural networks' learning performance cannot be used in the present case.
elements.sent-2411-756: Notably, notions such as Average Accuracy, Forward and Backward Transfer \cite{Lop2017, Cha2018 used in the field of Continual Learning to assess learning accuracy, generalization and resistance to catastrophic forgetting cannot be used.}
elements.sent-2411-757: Indeed, these metrics were devised to assess the performance of classifiers and are built upon a basic notion of accuracy that consists in checking whether two objects (generally the predicted class and the actual class of a sample item) are identical.
elements.sent-2411-758: But in the present proposal a prediction is not a single object but a set of objects (namely a set of predicted features), which is to be compared with another set of objects (the actual situation's set of features).
elements.sent-2411-759: Simply checking whether these two sets are identical would be a rather crude way to assess the prediction's accuracy: a more fine-grained comparison is needed, based on set inclusion properties.
elements.sent-2411-760: For these reasons, a suitable set of experiments and metrics was devised (see Section \ref{sec: Results ).}
elements.sent-2411-761: Each box represents a particular location in the agent's universe and possesses a particular set of features the agent is able to perceive, drawn from the set $\LF\ = \{$\OKFname, \KOFname, \NWallFname, \EWallFname, \SWallFname, \WWallFname, \ColdFname, \SoundFname, \CaseNbZeroFname, \CaseNbOneFname, ..., \CaseNbTwentyFourFname $ \}$.

elements.sent-2411-762: For example, the box with coordinates $(-2, -2)$ has the feature set $\LF_{(-2, -2) = \{$\OKFname, \SWallFname, \WWallFname, \ColdFname, \CaseNbZeroFname $ \}$, while the box with coordinates $(5, 0)$ has the feature set $\LF_{(5, 0) = \{$\KOFname$\}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-763: For $n \in \{1, ..., 24\}$, the expression ``\#n'' is to be taken as a particular name for a box.
elements.sent-2411-764: Some features are mutually exclusive, which means that they cannot pertain at the same time to a same box.
elements.sent-2411-765: Mutually exclusive features are \OKFname\ and \KOFname\ on the one hand, and boxes' names on the other.
elements.sent-2411-766: This means that a box cannot have both \OKFname\ and \KOFname\ in its feature set, and can have at most one name.
elements.sent-2411-767: For instance, Figure~\ref{fig: Universe .A shows the first room, which is composed of 25 boxes.}
elements.sent-2411-768: Opening a door amounts to removing the wall features from the corresponding boxes' feature sets (as in Figure~\ref{fig: Universe .B, where the \EWallFname\ feature has been removed from boxes $(2, -1)$, (2, 0) and $(2, 1)$).}
elements.sent-2411-769: Being able to handle these novelties (react appropriately and update its knowledge on the fly) is the agent's main challenge.
elements.sent-2411-770: Boxes' names in the first room are specific features (i.e., they apply to one single object).
elements.sent-2411-771: It is mainly used to increase the number of features boxes may have (from two to five), and also to check that the agent can form concepts with an intermediate degree of generality such as, e.g., the concept of cold OK boxes with a West Wall (see Section \ref{sec: Concepts and action laws for a formal definition of generality).}
elements.sent-2411-772: The set of the agent's possible motor activities is therefore the set $\MotAct = \{$North, North-East, East, South-East, South, South-West, West, North-West$\}$.
elements.sent-2411-773: The set of motor activity features the agent is able to perceive by proprioception is the set $\MF\ = \{$\NDeplFname, \NEDeplFname, \EDeplFname, \SEDeplFname, \SDeplFname, \SWDeplFname, \WDeplFname, \NWDeplFname, \DiagFname, \OrthFname$\}$, where the first eight are specific to each particular motor activity, while \DiagFname\ and \OrthFname\ are more general features shared by all motor activities yielding diagonal/orthogonal moves.
elements.sent-2411-774: These concepts bind together co-occurrent features and can be seen as some sort of conjunction in which conjuncts have different ``weights'', reflecting the fact that some features are more important than others in a concept's definition \cite{Fre08 .}
elements.sent-2411-775: They store the agent's knowledge about locations and more generally about any object, so we call them object concepts.
elements.sent-2411-776: These take other concepts as elements and bind them together into tuples.
elements.sent-2411-777: An object concept X is said to be general, as opposed to particular, if there is another concept Y such that the set of features composing X is a strict subset of the set of features composing Y.
elements.sent-2411-778: These definitions capture the fact that if X is more general than Y then the set of objects X applies to is a superset of the set of objects Y applies to.
elements.sent-2411-779: In line with this idea, in this work an action concept is said to be general if the object concept of its initial situation is general or its motor activity component only contains \DiagFname\ or \OrthFname\ (thus, if X and Y are action concepts such that X is more general than Y, then the set of actions X applies to is a superset of the set of actions Y applies to).
elements.sent-2411-780: Generality of both kinds of concepts is understood relatively to the set of concepts the agent possesses at some point, so no concept is general or particular in itself.
elements.sent-2411-781: Such general concepts capture the general (non-monotonic) action laws of the agent's universe.
elements.sent-2411-782: It is on them that the agent shall rely on to behave in never encountered situations.
elements.sent-2411-783: It should be mentioned that this representation of action laws is slightly different from the one used in the AI field of planning (see e.g.\ \cite{DBLP:journals/ai/FikesN71 ), which involves a notion of executability that specifies the conditions that need to hold for an action to be possible.}
elements.sent-2411-784: Yet the notion of executability can readily be recovered from action concepts by stating that the action represented by the action concept \cpt{x,\,y,\,z is executable (up to the agent's knowledge) in a situation $s$ if $s$ satisfies all the features composing \cptFeat{x and \mbox{\cptFeat{z $\neq$ \cpt{Failure .}}}}}
elements.section-2411-018: Implementing the agent's semantic memory in the neural network: overview
elements.sent-2411-785: This section presents the neural network implementing the agent's semantic memory.
elements.sent-2411-786: It first gives an overview of SNNs, and more particularly of the neural models and the learning rules which are relevant for that purpose.
elements.sent-2411-787: Then it presents the network's general architecture.
elements.sent-2411-788: Spiking Neural Networks (SNNs) are artificial neural networks in which information is transmitted between neurons by the means of spikes fired by neurons at other neurons.
elements.sent-2411-789: At each time step the activation level of each neuron is computed as a function of both its previous activation level and the amount of activation received by the neuron at this time step.
elements.sent-2411-790: If this activation level reaches a certain threshold (the neuron's spike threshold) the neuron fires a spike and its activation level is reset to some base value.
elements.sent-2411-791: Emitted spikes are conveyed from one neuron to another through weighted connections that modulate the amount of input transmitted by spikes to the receiving neuron.
elements.sent-2411-792: Learning consists in adjusting these weights.
elements.sent-2411-793: SNNs are well suited for autonomous learning in open worlds because they allow for Spike Time Dependent Plasticity (STDP), a family of biologically plausible learning rules which can achieve unsupervised online learning from unlabelled data \cite{ThiEtAl18 .}
elements.sent-2411-794: There are a number of STDP variants, but all have in common that the modification of the connection's weight between two neurons depends on the relative timing of their spikes.
elements.sent-2411-795: In the most popular version, the connection between two neurons is reinforced if the input neuron spikes just before the output neuron; it is decreased if it spikes just after the output neuron; and it is left unchanged otherwise.
elements.sent-2411-796: There are also many different implementations of STDP in SNNs, ranging from highly detailed and biologically accurate models to drastically simplified ones.
elements.sent-2411-797: Simplified versions are known for being rapid and energy-efficient \cite{YamEtAl2022 , which are interesting properties for autonomous robots.}
elements.sent-2411-798: The JAST learning rule \cite{ThoEtAl19,Tor23 is certainly one of the simplest STDP implementations one can find.}
elements.sent-2411-799: In this family of models, connections between neurons are binary (i.e., weights are either 0---meaning no connection---or 1) and each neuron has a fixed number of incoming connections.
elements.sent-2411-800: Learning is achieved through the replacement, at each time step, of a number $n_{swap}$ of inactive connections (i.e., connections from currently inactive input neurons) with an equal number of connections from unconnected active input neurons.
elements.sent-2411-801: Each neuron $i$ has two different thresholds $T_{fire}$ and $T_{learn (i)$, which respectively decide whether the neuron spikes and learns.}
elements.sent-2411-802: It is then fixed at this same value, so $T_{learn (i) \leq T_{fire}$ in any case.}
elements.sent-2411-803: The number $n_{swap (i)$ of connections to replace is initialized at some maximal value and is then decreased each time $i$ learns, until $n_{swap (i) = 0$ when $T_{learn (i) = T_{fire}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-804: This mechanism ``freezes'' the neuron after learning, thus preventing it from forgetting the acquired knowledge.
elements.sent-2411-805: In another version of JAST the spiking threshold $T_{fire}$ is not fixed but is dynamically adjusted so that a number $n$ of neurons (the ``n-best'' neurons) spike at each step.
elements.sent-2411-806: Although this learning rule is appealing for its simplicity, it cannot be used as it is for the present purpose.
elements.sent-2411-807: Obviously, freezing neurons is not suitable for continual learning and updating.
elements.sent-2411-808: Furthermore, neurons having both binary connections and a fixed number thereof would be unable to encode concepts having a variable number of features, which is required since an object's number of features may vary over time (for instance, when the door is closed the box $(2, 0)$ has the feature set $\LF_{(2, 0) = \{$\OKFname, \CaseNbTwentyTwoFname, \EWallFname$\}$, but this last feature is lost when the door opens).}
elements.sent-2411-809: More generally, online learning and updating in a changing world presents challenges to neural networks that seem difficult to overcome while sticking to JAST's simple setup.
elements.sent-2411-810: For this reason, the neural network implementing the agent’s semantic memory only retains some of the JAST learning rule's ideas, while relying on a more refined modeling of the neurons' internal dynamics to address these issues.
elements.sent-2411-811: Retained ideas are mainly the use of binary synapses, and the fact that learning consists in swapping a number of these so that the sum of the connections' weights on any given neuron remains constant through learning.
elements.sent-2411-812: This character ensures that there can be no ``dead'' neurons, i.e., neurons having lost most of their incoming connections and which are therefore unable to respond to any input.
elements.sent-2411-813: However here a same input neuron may have multiple synapses onto a same output neuron, which means that the connections' weights are in fact integers.
elements.sent-2411-814: Another retained idea is the use, in some specific cases, of dynamic spiking thresholds, to ensure that a given input always has a number of neurons responding to it.
elements.sent-2411-815: The paper's original contribution regarding SNNs mainly concerns the use of some internal metrics of the neurons to regulate learning and modulate information transmission.
elements.sent-2411-816: For instance, the input neurons' ability to establish new synapses onto output neurons depends on the number of synapses they already have, which facilitates the learning of rare or new features (such as e.g. \SoundFname).
elements.sent-2411-817: Another example is that the choice of the neurons recruited for learning a new concept depends on the time of their last spike.
elements.sent-2411-818: This allows to preferentially select less used neurons for new learning and to prevent in this manner the forgetting of more used---and therefore presumably more useful---knowledge.
elements.sent-2411-819: A third example is the modulation of information transmission between neurons in the querying process, which depends on the weight sum of input neurons' connections onto output neurons.
elements.sent-2411-820: This modulation favours the transmission of more specific information over that of less specific information, which is critical in non-monotonic contexts (see Section \ref{sec:Querying ).}
elements.sent-2411-821: It should be remarked that these methods do not depend on the specific type of spiking neurons used in the proposal and could thus be applied to regulate learning and forgetting and to balance information retrieval in other contexts.
elements.figure-2411-029: \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{Figure_3.pdf} \caption{Schema of the SNN. O-neurons \#1 and \#2 respectively encode the concepts \cpt{\#0,\,Cold,\,SouthWall,\,WestWall, \,OK} and \cpt{\#5,\,SouthWall,\,KO}. A-neuron \#1 encodes the concept \cpt{\cpt{\#0,\,Cold,\,SouthWall,\,WestWall,\,OK},\,\cpt{E,\,Orth}, \,\cpt{\#5,\,SouthWall,\,KO}}. A-neuron \#3 encodes the concept \cpt{\cpt{OK},\,\cpt{Diag},\,\cpt{OK}}. For graphical readability, connections weights are omitted. } \label{fig:SNN}
elements.sent-2411-822: For simplicity these reciprocal connections are not modelled as such, but instead information is allowed to flow in both directions along the same connections: from interface neurons to O-neurons and from there to A-neurons for learning and querying, and the other way round for retrieving information.
elements.sent-2411-823: I-neurons encoding mutually exclusive features have fixed reciprocal inhibitory connections which prevents them from firing together in a same computing process: as soon as one has fired, the others are shut down for the rest of the process.
elements.sent-2411-824: The $\FailureNeuron$ neuron has reciprocal inhibitory connections with each and any of the L-neurons.
elements.sent-2411-825: The first layer of hidden neurons (O-neurons) is composed of 100 neurons with a differentiated dynamics depending on whether their input source is I-neurons or A-neurons.
elements.sent-2411-826: The first compartment receives connections from O-neurons, the second from motor activities I-neurons, and the third from O-neurons and the $\FailureNeuron$ neuron.
elements.sent-2411-827: The use of compartment neurons to learn sequences of inputs was suggested in \cite{Cui16,HawEtAl16 .}
elements.sent-2411-828: This section describes the neural network's functioning and details its implementation.
elements.sent-2411-829: For readability, inessential algorithms and parameters settings are relegated in Appendices.
elements.sent-2411-830: Let \L, \M, and \F\ be the sets of interface neurons that respectively encode locations' features, motor activities' features, and failure (note that \F\ is the singleton $\{\FailureNeuron \}$).
elements.sent-2411-831: The sets of O- and A-neurons are respectively noted \O\ and \A .
elements.sent-2411-832: For convenience the neurons from \L\ are called ``L-neurons'', and similarly for the other sets.
elements.sent-2411-833: Synapses between any two neurons always have a weight of $1$, but an input neuron may have multiple synapses onto a same output neuron, so the weight of a connection between two neurons is a positive integer.
elements.sent-2411-834: By a slight abuse of language, when there is no synapse connecting two neurons we say that the weight of their connection is 0.
elements.sent-2411-835: Connections between the above sets of neurons are stored in separate arrays $\cnxLO$, $\cnxOAi$, $\cnxMAii$ and $\cnxOAiii$, where $\cnxLO$ reads ``connections from \L\ to \O'', $\cnxOAi$ reads ``connections from \O\ to \A's first compartment'', and similarly for the others.
elements.sent-2411-836: These arrays are initialized at random, in such a way that for any O-neuron $o$ \begin{equation}\sum \cnxLO[l][o]~for~l \in \L = \WSO, \intertext{and for any A-neuron $a$} \sum \cnxOAi[o][a]~for~o\in \O = \WSA, \sum \cnxMAii[m][a]~for~m \in \M = \WSA, \sum \cnxOAiii[n][a]~for~n \in \O \cup \F = \WSA,\end{equation} where $\WSO = 40$ and $\WSA = 30$.
elements.sent-2411-837: These values were chosen so as to allow enough redundancy between synapses to ensure robustness of learning while keeping the computational complexity as low as possible.
elements.sent-2411-838: Each O-neuron $o$ has two spike thresholds.
elements.sent-2411-839: The first one is noted $\STOo$ and governs its forward spiking, that is, the cases where its received input originates from L-neurons.
elements.sent-2411-840: The second is noted $\backwardSTOo$ and governs its backward spiking, that is, the cases where the input originates from A-neurons.
elements.sent-2411-842: These spike thresholds are stored in separate vectors $\STO$ and $\backwardSTO$ of length $\card{\O}$.
elements.sent-2411-843: Differentiated forward spike thresholds favour the encoding of concepts with different degrees of generality, as neurons with lower spike thresholds tend to spike more easily in response to inputs that do not perfectly fit their connections, which brings them to learn more general concepts.
elements.sent-2411-844: Lower backward spike thresholds eases information retrieval.
elements.sent-2411-845: L-neurons all have the same spike threshold $\STL = 50$, and the $\FailureNeuron$ neuron has its own spike threshold $\STFail = 40$.
elements.sent-2411-846: Spike thresholds for A-neurons are dynamically adjusted, in a way that is described in Section \ref{sec: A-neurons below.}
elements.sent-2411-847: The number of steps performed by the agent since the last spike of each O- and A-neuron is stored in separate vectors $\lastSpikedO$ and $\lastSpikedA$ of respective lengths $\card{\O}$ and $\card{\A}$.
elements.sent-2411-848: These vectors are initialized at random with values comprised between 1 and some upper bound.
elements.sent-2411-849: Last spike values are then increased by 1 at each step and reset to 0 each time the corresponding neuron spikes.
elements.sent-2411-850: Only the spikes that occur after a learning episode induce a reset, as they are the ones that indicate a successful encoding of the input.
elements.sent-2411-851: Notably, spikes occurring in the course of the querying process do not induce a reset.
elements.sent-2411-852: While the number of incoming synapses on O- and A-neurons is strictly constrained by $\WSO$ and $\WSA$, the number of outgoing synapses from I- and O- neurons is softly constrained by Synpases Growth Rates ($\SGR$s).
elements.sent-2411-853: For any neuron $n$ from \I\ or \O, $n$'s $\SGR$ is a decreasing function of the number of outgoing synapses $n$ has at the moment.
elements.sent-2411-854: In the algorithms provided in the sequel we introduce and use dictionary structures in which each value $v$ can be accessed through an index (``key'') $k$.
elements.sent-2411-855: Formally, a dictionary can be seen as a set of pairs $(k, v)$ such that for any pairs $(k, v)$ and $(k', v')$ in $D$, $k \neq k'$.
elements.sent-2411-856: If $D$ is a dictionary, then the operation $D[k] \leftarrow v$ means that the value $v$ is added at index $k$ in $D$.
elements.sent-2411-857: If $k$ already exists in $D$ then its value is overwritten.
elements.sent-2411-858: If $S$ is a subset of $D$'s indices, the operation $\mathit{RemoveByKey (D, S)$ modifies $D$ by removing all its elements having their indices in $S$.}
elements.sent-2411-859: That is, $\mathit{RemoveByKey (D, S) = D \setminus \{ (k,v) \in D \mid k \in S \}$.}
elements.sent-2411-860: We also use a function $\argmaxD$ which given a dictionary $D$ returns the subset of its indices with maximal values (that is, if $x = \max(\{v \mid (k,v) \in D\})$, then $\argmaxD(D) = \{k \mid (k,x) \in D\}$).
elements.sent-2411-861: We also use Python's Numpy function \randomChoice$(a, n, \mathit{replace}$, $p)$, where $a$ is a set, a vector or an array, and the other parameters are optional with default values: \begin{compactitem \item[-] $n$ is the number of elements to return (default is $1)$.}
elements.sent-2411-862: If $n$ is not specified, \randomChoice\ does not return a vector $[v]$ but a single value $v$.
elements.sent-2411-863: The set of L-neurons that fire in response to an input from either the perceptual system (if the agent is observing its current location) or the decision system (if the agent is querying its semantic memory) is noted $\inputLO$.
elements.sent-2411-864: The set of O-neurons that fire in response to $\inputLO$\ is noted $\firedO$.
elements.sent-2411-865: It is computed as follows (see Algorithm \ref{spikeO ).}
elements.algorithm-2411-001: \caption{Function \spikeO\ (forward spiking of O-neurons)}\label{spikeO} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \Input $(\inputLO, \cnxLO)$ \LComment{Note that: $\inputLO \subseteq \L$, $cnxLO$ is a $(\card{\L} \times \card{\O})$ matrix of integers, $\STO$ is a vector of integers of size $\card{\O}$, $\TnbFiredO$ is a constant (the maximum desired number of firing neurons).} \State $\firedO \gets \emptyset$ \Comment{the set of neurons that fired} \State $\inputSumO \gets [0, ..., 0]$ \Comment{vector of integers of size $\card{\O}$} \Comment{we note $\inputSumO[o]$ the value of $\inputSumO$ at position $o$} \State $\aboveT \gets \emptyset$ \Comment{dictionary with keys in \O\ and values in $\mathbb{N}$} \For{$o \in$ \O} \State $\inputSumO[o] \gets \sum_{l \in \inputLO}\cnxLO[l][o]$ \Comment{amount of activation received by neuron o} \If{$\inputSumO[o] > \STOo$} \State $\aboveT[o] \gets \inputSumO[o] - \STOo $ \EndIf \EndFor \While{$\card{\firedO} < \TnbFiredO and \aboveT \neq \emptyset$} \State $S \gets \argmaxD(\aboveT)$ \Comment{the set of O-neurons with maximal over threshold activation value} \State $\firedO \gets \firedO \cup S$ \State $\aboveT \gets \RemoveByKey(\aboveT, S)$ \EndWhile \Statex \Return $(\inputSumO,\, \firedO)$ \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-866: First, for any neuron $o$ in \O\ the sum $\inputSumO[o]$ of inputs received by $o$ is computed.
elements.sent-2411-867: O-neurons for which $\inputSumO[o]$ is above their spike threshold $\STOo$ then fire in turn, starting by those with highest difference with their spike threshold, until a target number $\TnbFiredO$ of firing neurons is reached or no neuron reaching its spike threshold remains.
elements.sent-2411-868: Given a set $\firedO$ of firing O-neurons, the information stored by these neurons can be retrieved by the backward firing of L-neurons.
elements.sent-2411-869: This consists in having the O-neurons from $\firedO$ send an input to L-neurons to make them spike (see Algorithm \ref{backwardSpikeL in Appendices).}
elements.sent-2411-870: The process is similar as above, except that in order to suppress noise only the connections having a weight above a noise threshold $\noiseO$ transmit the input.
elements.sent-2411-871: Furthermore, mutual inhibition between \L-neurons encoding mutually exclusive features makes that once a \L-neuron encoding a feature $f$ has fired, all the \L--neurons encoding features $f'$ excluded by $f$ are prevented from firing.
elements.sent-2411-872: Learning of object concepts (see Algorithm \ref{learnO ) occurs after each observation by the agent of its current situation.}
elements.sent-2411-873: The set of O-neurons selected for learning is noted $\learningO$.
elements.sent-2411-874: These are primarily the neurons that fired at observation time, that is, the neurons from $\firedO$.
elements.algorithm-2411-002: \caption{Function \learnO\ (O-neurons' learning)}\label{learnO} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \Input $(\inputLO, \inputSumO, \firedO, \cnxLO, \lastSpikedO)$ \LComment{Recall that: $\inputLO \subseteq \L$ and $firedO \subset \O$; $\inputSumO$ and $lastSpikedO$ are vectors of integers of size $\card{\O}$; $\cnxLO$ is a $(\card{\L} \times \card{\O})$ matrix of integers; $\TnbLearningO$ is a constant (the minimum desired number of learning O-neurons).} \State $\learningO\ \gets \firedO$ \If {$\card{\learningO} < \TnbLearningO$} \State $\boostedInputSumO \gets \hyperref[boostInputLO]{\boostInputLO} (\learningO, \inputSumO, \lastSpikedO)$ \While{$\card{\learningO} < \TnbLearningO and \boostedInputSumO \neq\emptyset$} \State $\addedNeurons \gets \argmaxD(\boostedInputSumO)$ \Comment{subset of $\O$} \State $\learningO \gets \learningO \cup \addedNeurons$ \State $\boostedInputSumO \gets \RemoveByKey(\boostedInputSumO, \addedNeurons)$ \EndWhile \EndIf \State $\probaNewSynapsesLO \gets$ \hyperref[calculateProbaNewSynapsesLO]{\calculateProbaNewSynapsesLO}$(\inputLO,$ $ \cnxLO)$ \State $\firedL \gets \hyperref[backwardSpikeL]{\backwardSpikeL}(\firedO, \cnxLO)$ \Comment{$\firedL$ is a set of firing L-neurons} \If{$\inputLO \subseteq \firedL$} \State $\cnxLO \gets \hyperref[replaceAllInactiveSynapses]{\replaceAllInactiveSynapses} (\learningO, \inputLO, \cnxLO, \L, $ $\probaNewSynapsesLO)$ \Else \If{$\card{\learningO} < \TnbFiredO$} \State $\boostedInputSumO \gets \hyperref[boostInputLO]{\boostInputLO} (\learningO, \inputSumO, \lastSpikedO)$ \State $\learningO \gets \learningO \cup \argmaxD(\boostedInputSumO)$ \EndIf \State $\maxLastSpike \gets elements.algorithm-2411-003: \caption{Function \boostInputLO\ (boosts the input from \L\ to O-neurons)} \label{boostInputLO} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \Input $(\learningO, \inputSumO, \lastSpikedO)$ \LComment{Recall that: $\learningO \subseteq \O$; $\inputSumO$ and $\lastSpikedO $ are vectors of integers of size $\card{\O}$.} \State $\boostedInputSumO \gets \emptyset$ \Comment{dictionary with keys in \O\ and values in $\mathbb{R}$} \For{$o \in \O \setminus \learningO$} \State $\boost \gets (\lastSpikedO[o] + \boostParamO) / \boostParamO )$ \Comment{$\boostParamO = 50$ is a constant; it determines the slope of the function's curve.} \State $\boostedInputSumO[o] \gets \inputSumO[o] * \boost$ \EndFor \Statex \Return $\boostedInputSumO$ \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-878: In practice, for each non-firing O-neuron a boosting coefficient $\boost$ is calculated, which is an increasing function of $\lastSpikedO[o]$.
elements.sent-2411-879: Neurons with the highest boosted input sums are then successively added to $\learningO$ until $\TnbLearningO$ is reached or no more neurons can be found.
elements.sent-2411-880: As neurons that did not spike for a long time receive more boosting, they tend to be preferentially recruited in this process.
elements.sent-2411-881: This means that the network preferentially selects neurons encoding less used knowledge to learn new information.

elements.sent-2411-884: To ensure that boosting only occurs when very few O-neurons fire in response to the input, $\TnbLearningO$ is set to $\TnbFiredO/2$ ($\TnbFiredO =12$ and $\TnbLearningO = 6$).

elements.algorithm-2411-004: \caption{\calculateProbaNewSynapsesLO\ (calculates L-neurons' probability to establish new synapses onto O-neurons)}\label{calculateProbaNewSynapsesLO} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \Input $(\inputLO, \cnxLO)$ \LComment{Recall that: $\inputLO \subseteq \L$; $\cnxLO$ is a $(\card{\L} \times \card{\O)}$ matrix of integers.} \State $\SGR, \probaNewSynapsesLO \gets \emptyset$ \Comment{dictionaries with keys in $\inputLO$ and values in $\mathbb{R}$.} \For{$l \in \inputLO$} \State $\fwrdCnxSum \gets \sum_{o \in \O} \cnxLO[l][o]$ \State $\SGR[l] \gets \tanh( (-\fwrdCnxSum + a) / b ) + c )$ \LComment{$a = 300$, $b = 150$ and $c = 2$ are constants; they determine the horizontal offset, the slope and the vertical offset of the function's curve.} \EndFor \State $s \gets \sum_{l \in \inputLO} \SGR[l]$ \For{$l \in \inputLO$} \State $\probaNewSynapsesLO[l] \gets \SGR[l] / s$ \EndFor \Statex \Return $\probaNewSynapsesLO$ \end{algorithmic}

elements.sent-2411-921: It represents $a$'s propensity to modify its connections through learning, by specifying how many of its synapses should be at most/at least replaced in the learning process.
elements.sent-2411-922: This number is calculated in such a way that A-neurons with the highest $\lastSpikedA$ value get the maximal learning rate $\WSA$, those with the lowest $\lastSpikedA$ value get a learning rate of $1$, and the others get learning rates in between.
elements.sent-2411-923: The idea behind this is that neurons having not spiked for a long time should be more ready to modify their connections so to adjust to the precise current situation and thus get to encode particular concepts, while neurons that often spike should minimally change their connections each time they spike so to tune themselves to the common features shared by all these situations and thus get to encode general concepts.

elements.sent-2411-934: This section first explains how the agent queries its semantic memory to predict the outcomes of envisaged actions, and then how the agent uses these predictions to make its decisions.
elements.sent-2411-935: Finally, it describes the whole process of making a step.

elements.sent-2411-959: The process by which it decides which motor activity to perform (i.e., in which direction to go) is run by its decision system (see Algorithm \ref{chooseAMove ).}
elements.algorithm-2411-008: \caption{\chooseAMove\ (decides the agent's next move)}\label{chooseAMove} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \Input $(\inputOAi, \cnxOAi, \cnxMAii, \cnxOAiii, \cnxLO, \lastSpikedA)$ \LComment{Recall that: $\inputOAi \subset \O$; $\cnxOAi$, $\cnxMAii$, $\cnxOAiii$ and $\cnxLO$ are matrices of integers of respective dimensions \mbox{$(\card{\O} \times \card{\A})$, $(\card{\M} \times \card{\A})$, $(\card{\O \cup \F} \times \card{\A})$ and $(\card{\L} \times \card{\O})$;} $\lastSpikedA$ is a vector of integers of length $\card{\A}$.} \State $\selectedMode \gets$ \hyperref[randomChoice]{\randomChoice}$(\{$``Exploration'',``Exploitation''$\})$ \State $\predictions \gets$ \hyperref[makePredictions]{\makePredictions} $(\inputOAi, \cnxOAi, \cnxMAii, \cnxOAiii, \cnxLO, \lastSpikedA)$ \State $(\suitable, \unsuitable, \undecided) \gets$ \hyperref[ratePredictions]{\ratePredictions}$(\predictions)$ \State $\move \gets$ \hyperref[makeAChoice]{\makeAChoice}$(\suitable, \unsuitable, \undecided, \selectedMode)$ \State $\predictedFeatures \gets \{f \mid (f, \confi{f}) \in \predictions[\move]\}$ \Statex \Return $(\move, \predictedFeatures)$ \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-960: First, the agent decides whether to exploit its current knowledge about its environment, or to explore its environment to improve its knowledge.
elements.sent-2411-961: For this reason we do not try to reach an optimal solution here, but we simply make the agent's decision system choose at random with equal probability between an Exploration and an Exploitation mode.
elements.sent-2411-962: The agent then makes predictions about the outcome of each possible action.
elements.sent-2411-963: To do so, it successively queries its semantic memory for the outcome of the action having the current location as initial situation and one of the eight possible moves as motor activity (see Algorithm \ref{makePredictions ).}
elements.algorithm-2411-009: \caption{\makePredictions\ (makes predictions about the outcome of each motor activity given the current situation)}\label{makePredictions} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \Input $(\inputOAi, \cnxOAi, \cnxMAii, \cnxOAiii, \cnxLO, \lastSpikedA)$ \LComment{Recall that: $\inputOAi \subset \O$; $\cnxOAi$, $\cnxMAii$, $\cnxOAiii$ and $\cnxLO$ are matrices of integers of respective dimensions $(\card{\O} \times \card{\A})$, $(\card{\M} \times \card{\A})$, $(\card{\O \cup \F} \times \card{\A})$ and \mbox{$(\card{\L} \times \card{\O})$;} $\lastSpikedA$ is a vector of integers of length $\card{\A}$; $\MotAct$ is the set of the agent's possible motor activities and $\LF$ the set of locations' features; $\backwardFiredI$ is a dictionary whit keys in $\L \cup \F$ and values in $\mathbb{R}$.} \State $\predictions \gets \emptyset$ \Comment{dictionary with keys in $\MotAct$ and values which are themselves dictionaries with keys in $\LF\cup \{\FailFname\}$ and values in $\mathbb{R}$.} \For{$\move \in \MotAct$} \State $m \gets$ index of the M-neuron encoding the motor activity feature corresponding to $\move$. \State $m' \gets$ index of the M-neuron encoding the motor activity feature \DiagFname\ or \OrthFname, depending on whether $\move$ is a diagonal or orthogonal move. \State $\inputMAii \gets \{m,\, m'\}.$ \State $(\backwardFiredI, \firedA) \gets$ \hyperref[query]{\query}$(\inputOAi, \cnxOAi, \inputMAii, \cnxMAii, $ $\cnxOAiii, \cnxLO, \lastSpikedA)$ \State $\predictions[\move] \gets \emptyset$ \Comment{\mbox{dictionary with keys in $\LF \cup \{\FailFname\}$ and values in $\mathbb{R}$}} \If{$\backwardFiredI \neq \emptyset$} \State $\shortTermMemoryCoef \gets 1 + (1/\mean(\{\lastSpikedA[a] \mid a \in \firedA\}))$ \For{$i \in \{i \mid (i, \mathit{threshold}) \in \backwardFiredI\}$ } \State $f\gets$ feature encoded by $i$ \State $\confi{f}\gets \backwardFiredI[i]* \shortTermMemoryCoef$ \State $\predictions[\move][f] \gets \confi{f}$ \EndFor \EndIf \EndFor \Statex \Return $\predictions$ \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-964: After each such querying process, a coefficient $\shortTermMemoryCoef$ is computed from the $\lastSpikedA$ values of the A-neurons firing in the course of the query process.
elements.sent-2411-965: For each interface neuron $i$ firing as a result of the querying process, the feature $f$ encoded by $i$ is then retrieved, and a confidence degree $\confi{f}$ is computed from $\shortTermMemoryCoef$ and the A-neurons' spike threshold at the moment $i$ fired.
elements.sent-2411-966: It corresponds to the agent's estimation of how well the action concepts on which it relied to make its prediction fit the envisaged action, weighted by its short term memory.

(f_n, \confi{f_n})\}\end{equation} is itself a dictionary which to each feature $f_i$ predicted by the agent associates the degree of confidence $\confi{fi}$ the agent has in its prediction of $f_i$.
elements.sent-2411-968: The agent then rates each motor activity $\move$ for its suitability (see Algorithm \ref{ratePredictions in Appendices), by building the sets $\suitable$, $\unsuitable$ and $\undecided$ such that: \begin{equation}\suitable = \{(\move,\, \confi{OK}) \mid (\OKFname, \confi{OK}) \in \predictions[\move] \} \unsuitable \{(\move, \confi{f}) \mid (f = \KOFname or f = \FailFname) and (f, \, \confi{f}) \in \predictions[\move] \} \undecided = \{\move\mid \nexists \conf such that (\move,\conf) \in \suitable \cup \unsuitable\}\end{equation} Finally, the decision system chooses a motor activity depending on the selected mode (see Algorithm \ref{makeAChoice in Appendices).}}
elements.sent-2411-969: In Exploration mode, the agent is willing to take risks and chooses an action with the most uncertain outcome possible so to increase its knowledge: if $\undecided \neq \emptyset$ it picks one from it, otherwise it goes for one with the least $\conf$ in $\suitable\cup \unsuitable$.
elements.sent-2411-970: So, if $\suitable\neq\emptyset$ it chooses a move with the greatest $\conf$, otherwise if $\undecided\neq\emptyset$ it picks one from it, and if both $\suitable$ and $\undecided$ are empty it chooses a move with the least $\conf$ in $\unsuitable$.

elements.sent-2411-1026: It should be stressed that this architecture does not crucially depends on the specific type of spiking neurons used to implement it nor on the details of its implementation.
elements.sent-2411-1027: Any similar architecture based on a spiking neural network using some STDP-like learning rules would probably work, provided that it retains the approach's main ideas.
elements.sent-2411-1028: Notably, strict constraints on output neurons' connections weight sums associated with flexible constraints on input neurons' forward connections weight sums allow to regulate learning and keep the network balanced; target numbers of neurons for spiking and learning associated with dynamic learning thresholds ensure that the network will always have neurons to learn a new input; boosting the inputs received by the neurons having not spiked for a long time allows to preferentially select those encoding less used knowledge for learning new inputs, and to prevent in this manner catastrophic forgetting; forcing a few neurons to learn particular concepts counterbalances STDP's tendency towards generalization and favours the representation of concepts with diverse degrees of generality; finally, input modulation in the querying process promotes the firing of neurons encoding the most specific information, thus helping the agent to make accurate predictions in non-monotonic contexts.
elements.sent-2411-1029: All these methods rely on individual neurons' properties such as the weight sum of their incoming / forward connections, the time of their last spike and other similar metrics.
elements.sent-2411-1030: However, it is important to observe that these are upper bounds: the agent's ability to form general concepts out of individual experiences and to use them to make decisions makes that it is not necessary to encode each individual object with all its features and each performed action.
elements.sent-2411-1031: To do so, a notion of executable action law should be defined (see Section \ref{sec: Concepts and action laws ).}
elements.sent-2411-1032: The agent would also need to build its own set of possible situations (states) online (the set of its object concepts could probably be used to this end).
elements.sent-2411-1033: Finally, the decision system should be augmented to represent goals and embed a cost function and a planning algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-1034: It would also be useful to implement negation in the network to allow the agent to represent the fact that a given object does not have a given feature.
elements.sent-2411-1035: Intuitively, it seems that the choice between these two modes should depend on the agent's estimation of the risk associated with an explorative behaviour, and that the agent should refrain from entering in exploration mode in situations where the assessed risk is high, while allowing itself more exploration in situations where it is low.
